title.zh: 卷六十四下 嚴朱吾丘主父徐嚴終王賈傳
title.en: Volume 64b: an, Zhu, Wuqiu, Zhufu, Xu, Yan, Zhong, Wang and Jia 2
title.raw: 卷六十四下 嚴朱吾丘主父徐嚴終王賈傳 Volume 64b: an, Zhu, Wuqiu, Zhufu, Xu, Yan, Zhong, Wang and Jia 2

sentences.zh: 卷六十四下 嚴朱吾丘主父徐嚴終王賈傳 第三十三下

translations.literal: Volume 64b: Biographies of Yan, Zhu, Wuqiu, Zhufu, Xu, Yan, Zhong, Wang, and Jia, Part Two, Number 33b.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 64b, Part Two: biographies of Yan An, Zhu Mai Chen, Wuqiu Shouwang, Zhufu Yan, Xu Le, Yan Cang, Zhong Jun, Wang Bao, and Jia Yi—the thirty-third set of biographies, lower scroll.

sentences.zh: 嚴安者，臨菑人也。

translations.literal: Yan An was a man of Linzi.
translations.idiomatic: Yan An came from Linzi.

sentences.zh: 以故丞相史上書，曰：

translations.literal: In the capacity of a former clerk of the chancellor, he submitted a memorial, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Serving as a clerk who had once worked under the chancellor, he presented a memorial that began:

sentences.zh: 臣聞鄒衍曰：「政教文質者，所以云救也，當時則用，過則舍之，有易則易也，故守一而不變者，未睹治之至也。」

translations.literal: Your servant has heard Zou Yan say: 'Government and instruction, culture and substance—these are means by which one speaks of remedy; what fits the times is employed, what has passed is set aside; when there is something to change, one changes it; therefore one who holds fast to one thing and does not alter has never seen the utmost of good order.'
translations.idiomatic: I have heard Zou Yan say that policy and moral teaching, refinement and plainness, exist to redress imbalance: use what suits the moment, discard what is out of date, and change when change is due. A ruler who clings to a single formula and never revises it has never grasped what true good government looks like.

sentences.zh: 今天下人民用財侈靡，車馬衣裘宮室皆競修飾，調五聲使有節族，雜五色使有文章，重五味方丈於前，以觀欲天下。

translations.literal: Now the people of the realm in their use of wealth are extravagant; carriages, horses, garments, furs, and palaces all vie in ornament; they tune the five tones so that they have rhythm, mix the five colors so that they have pattern, pile the five flavors a full square before them, in order to display desire to the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Today the common people spend money with reckless luxury: they outdo one another in decking out carriages, horses, robes, furs, and houses; they arrange the five notes into elaborate music, blend the five colors into rich designs, and heap rare flavors across a table before them—all to flaunt appetite and set a fashion for the whole world.

sentences.zh: 彼民之情，見美則願之，是教民以侈也。

translations.literal: As for the disposition of the people, when they see beauty they desire it; this is teaching the people extravagance.
translations.idiomatic: Human nature being what it is, people covet whatever looks splendid; in effect, the court is schooling them in excess.

sentences.zh: 侈而無節，則不可贍，民離本而徼末矣。

translations.literal: If there is extravagance without restraint, then it cannot be supplied; the people leave the root and pursue the branch.
translations.idiomatic: Extravagance without limits outruns any purse, and the people abandon farming to chase petty profit at the margins.

sentences.zh: 末不可徒得，故搢紳者不憚為詐，帶劍者夸殺人以矯奪，而世不知媿，故姦軌浸長。

translations.literal: The branch cannot be obtained in vain; therefore girdled gentlemen do not shrink from deceit; sword-bearing men boast of killing people in order to seize by force, and the age does not know shame; therefore villainy gradually grows.
translations.idiomatic: Those secondary gains do not come honestly, so belted officials turn to fraud without a qualm, and men who wear swords swagger through murder and robbery, while society feels no shame. Crime and violence spread unchecked.

sentences.zh: 夫佳麗珍怪固順於耳目，故養失而泰，樂失而淫，禮失而采，教失而偽。

translations.literal: Now beauties and rare curiosities indeed please ear and eye; therefore nurturing misses and becomes excessive, music misses and becomes dissolute, ritual misses and becomes ornamental, instruction misses and becomes false.
translations.idiomatic: Splendid sights and rare treasures naturally delight the senses, yet when we indulge them, nourishment slides into excess, music into licentiousness, ritual into mere display, and moral teaching into hollow pretense.

sentences.zh: 偽、采、淫、泰，非所以範民之道也。

translations.literal: Falsehood, ornament, dissoluteness, excess—these are not the way to model the people.
translations.idiomatic: Pretense, ornament, debauchery, and extravagance are no model for how the people ought to live.

sentences.zh: 是以天下人民逐利無已，犯法者眾。

translations.literal: Therefore the people of the realm pursue profit without end, and those who violate the law are many.
translations.idiomatic: The result is a tireless scramble for gain across the land, and the jails fill with lawbreakers.

sentences.zh: 臣願為民制度以防其淫，使貧富不相燿以和其心。

translations.literal: Your servant wishes to institute regulations for the people in order to guard against their excess, so that poor and rich do not dazzle one another and thereby harmonize their hearts.
translations.idiomatic: I urge you to set norms that curb excess, so that rich and poor are not locked in mutual envy and hearts can find peace.

sentences.zh: 心既和平，其性恬安。

translations.literal: When the heart is already harmonious and peaceful, their nature is tranquil and secure.
translations.idiomatic: When minds are at peace, temperaments grow calm and steady.

sentences.zh: 恬安不營，則盜賊銷；

translations.literal: If tranquil and secure they do not scheme, then bandits and thieves disappear.
translations.idiomatic: Content people who are not driven by restless ambition will not turn to theft.

sentences.zh: 盜賊銷，則刑罰少；

translations.literal: If bandits and thieves disappear, then punishments are few.
translations.idiomatic: With theft rare, punishments become infrequent.

sentences.zh: 刑罰少，則陰陽和，四時正，風雨時，草木暢茂，五穀蕃孰，六畜遂字，民不夭厲，和之至也。

translations.literal: If punishments are few, then yin and yang are harmonious, the four seasons are correct, wind and rain come in season, plants and trees flourish luxuriantly, the five grains ripen abundantly, the six kinds of livestock breed as they should, and the people do not die young from pestilence—this is the utmost of harmony.
translations.idiomatic: Fewer punishments mean balanced yin and yang, orderly seasons, timely rain and wind, lush growth, abundant harvests, thriving herds, and no plague of untimely deaths—this is harmony brought to perfection.

sentences.zh: 臣聞周有天下，其治三百餘歲，成康其隆也，刑錯四十餘年而不用。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that when Zhou possessed the realm, its government was more than three hundred years; Cheng and Kang were its height, and punishments were set aside for more than forty years without being used.
translations.idiomatic: I am told the Zhou held the realm for over three hundred years of rule; the reigns of King Cheng and King Kang were its high point, when criminal penalties lay unused for more than forty years.

sentences.zh: 及其衰，亦三百餘年，故五伯更起。

translations.literal: When it came to its decline, it was also more than three hundred years; therefore the Five Hegemons arose in succession.
translations.idiomatic: Its decline lasted another three hundred years, a span in which the Five Lords Protector rose one after another.

sentences.zh: 伯者，常佐天子興利除害，誅暴禁邪，匡正海內，以尊天子。

translations.literal: The hegemons constantly assisted the Son of Heaven in raising benefit and removing harm, punishing violence and prohibiting evil, rectifying within the seas, in order to honor the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Those hegemons habitually aided the Son of Heaven by advancing good, removing harm, crushing cruelty, and checking wickedness, bringing order within the Four Seas so as to uphold the throne.

sentences.zh: 五伯既沒，賢聖莫續，天子孤弱，號令不行。

translations.literal: When the Five Hegemons were already gone, worthy and sage men did not continue; the Son of Heaven was isolated and weak, and commands did not go forth.
translations.idiomatic: After the last hegemon died, no worthy successor appeared; the Zhou king grew isolated and feeble, and his decrees carried no weight.

sentences.zh: 諸侯恣行，彊陵弱，眾暴寡。

translations.literal: The feudal lords acted as they pleased; the strong encroached on the weak, the many tyrannized the few.
translations.idiomatic: Regional lords did as they pleased: the strong swallowed the weak, and great states bullied small ones.

sentences.zh: 田常篡齊，六卿分晉，並為戰國，此民之始苦也。

translations.literal: Tian Chang usurped Qi, the six ministers divided Jin, and together they became the Warring States—this was the beginning of the people's suffering.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Chang seized Qi, six noble clans carved up Jin, and the age of the Warring States began—the first great wave of misery for the common people.

sentences.zh: 於是彊國務攻，弱國修守，合從連衡，馳車轂擊，介冑生蟣蝨，民無所告愬。

translations.literal: Thereupon strong states devoted themselves to attack, weak states cultivated defense; vertical and horizontal alliances; racing chariots, hub striking hub; armor and helmets bred lice; the people had nowhere to lodge complaints.
translations.idiomatic: Mighty states threw everything into conquest while weaker ones dug in behind their walls; leagues shifted from north–south pacts to east–west deals; chariots jammed wheel-hub to wheel-hub; soldiers lived so long in mail that lice bred in the lining; ordinary folk had no court to which they could appeal.

sentences.zh: 及至秦王，蠶食天下，并吞戰國，稱號皇帝，一海內之政，壞諸侯之城。

translations.literal: When it reached the King of Qin, he nibbled away the realm, swallowed the Warring States, styled himself emperor, unified government within the seas, and destroyed the cities of the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: Then came the First Emperor of Qin, who gnawed away the states one by one, swallowed the warring kingdoms, took the title of emperor, centralized all authority, and razed the walls of the old feudatories.

sentences.zh: 銷其兵，鑄以為鍾虡，示不復用。

translations.literal: He melted their weapons and cast them into bells and bell-stands, showing that they would not be used again.
translations.idiomatic: He had their bronze weapons melted down and recast into bells and bell-frames, a public pledge that arms would not be taken up again.

sentences.zh: 元元黎民得免於戰國，逢明天子，人人自以為更生。

translations.literal: The black-haired common people were able to escape the Warring States period; they encountered a bright enlightened Son of Heaven; each person thought himself reborn.
translations.idiomatic: The common people, delivered at last from endless war, believed they had met an enlightened sovereign; every household felt as though it had been given a new life.

sentences.zh: 鄉使秦緩刑罰，薄賦斂，省繇役，貴仁義，賤權利，上篤厚，下佞巧，變風易俗，化於海內，則世世必安矣。

translations.literal: If formerly Qin had relaxed punishments, lightened taxes and levies, reduced corvée labor, honored benevolence and rightness, despised power and profit, made superiors honest and thick, and inferiors not crafty and clever, changed customs and altered practices, and transformed within the seas, then generation after generation would surely have been secure.
translations.idiomatic: Had Qin only eased its penalties, cut taxes and labor service, honored humanity and duty over ruthless calculation, encouraged integrity in high places and discouraged sycophancy below, and reformed customs throughout the realm, its peace might have lasted for generations.

sentences.zh: 秦不行是風，循其故俗，為知巧權利者進，篤厚忠正者退，法嚴令苛，諂諛者眾，曰聞其美，章廣心逸。

translations.literal: Qin did not practice this wind; it followed its old customs; those who were clever in power and profit advanced, those who were honest, thick, loyal, and upright retreated; laws were strict and orders harsh; flatterers were many; daily they heard praise of its beauty; splendor was broad and the heart was dissipated.
translations.idiomatic: Qin did nothing of the kind. It clung to old habits, promoting the cunning and the self-seeking while sidelining the steadfast and true. Statutes grew harsher, orders more cruel, and flatterers multiplied; the court daily heard only its own praises until ambition swelled and discipline collapsed.

sentences.zh: 欲威海外，使蒙恬將兵以北攻彊胡，辟地進境，戍於北河，飛芻輓粟以隨其後。

translations.literal: Desiring to awe beyond the seas, he sent Meng Tian leading troops to attack northward the strong Hu, opened land and advanced the border, garrisoned at the Northern River, and flew fodder and hauled grain to follow behind.
translations.idiomatic: Intent on striking terror into lands beyond China, he sent Meng Tian north against the Xiongnu, pushed the frontier forward, planted garrisons along the northern river line, and then rushed grain and fodder north in an endless train to supply them.

sentences.zh: 又使尉屠睢將樓船之士攻越，使監祿鑿渠運糧，深入越地，越人遁逃。

translations.literal: He also sent Commandant Tu Sui leading tower-ship troops to attack Yue, sent Supervisor Lu to dig a canal to transport grain, deeply entered Yue territory, and the Yue people fled.
translations.idiomatic: He also ordered Commandant Tu Sui to take marines in tower ships against the Yue, while Superintendent Lu cut a canal to move supplies deep into Yue country until the local people melted away into the hills.

sentences.zh: 曠日持久，糧食乏絕，越人擊之，秦兵大敗。

translations.literal: Days dragged on and time was prolonged; grain supplies were exhausted; the Yue people struck them, and Qin troops were greatly defeated.
translations.idiomatic: The campaign dragged on until provisions ran out; the Yue struck back and shattered the Qin army.

sentences.zh: 秦乃使尉佗將卒以戍越。

translations.literal: Qin then sent Commandant Tuo leading troops to garrison Yue.
translations.idiomatic: Qin then dispatched Commandant Zhao Tuo with a garrison to hold the conquered south.

sentences.zh: 當是時，秦禍北構於胡，南挂於越，宿兵於無用之地，進而不得退。

translations.literal: At this time, Qin's disaster was constructed north with the Hu and hung south with Yue; it lodged troops in useless places, advancing but unable to retreat.
translations.idiomatic: At that moment Qin was caught between the Xiongnu in the north and Yue in the south, with armies tied down in barren country where they could advance but not withdraw.

sentences.zh: 行十餘年，丁男被甲，丁女轉輸，苦不聊生，自經於道樹，死者相望。

translations.literal: For more than ten years it went on; adult males donned armor, adult females transported supplies; suffering, they had no means to live; they hanged themselves on trees along the roads; the dead faced one another.
translations.idiomatic: For over a decade every able-bodied man wore armor and every woman hauled supplies for the front. Life became unbearable; people strung themselves up from roadside trees until the corpses lined the highways.

sentences.zh: 及秦皇帝崩，天下大畔。

translations.literal: When the Qin emperor died, the realm greatly rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: When the First Emperor died, the empire erupted in revolt.

sentences.zh: 陳勝、吳廣舉陳，武臣、張耳舉趙，項梁舉吳，田儋舉齊，景駒舉郢，周市舉魏，韓廣舉燕，窮山通谷，豪士並起，不可勝載也。

translations.literal: Chen Sheng and Wu Guang raised Chen; Wu Chen and Zhang Er raised Zhao; Xiang Liang raised Wu; Tian Dan raised Qi; Jing Ju raised Ying; Zhou Shi raised Wei; Han Guang raised Yan; through exhausted mountains and connecting valleys, bold men rose together—their number cannot be fully recorded.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Sheng and Wu Guang seized Chen, Wu Chen and Zhang Er raised Zhao, Xiang Liang rose in Wu, Tian Dan in Qi, Jing Ju in Ying, Zhou Shi in Wei, Han Guang in Yan—bold leaders sprang up in every ravine and pass, too many to list.

sentences.zh: 然本皆非公侯之後，非長官之吏，無尺寸之勢，起閭巷，杖棘矜，應時而動，不謀而俱起，不約而同會，壤長地進，至乎伯王，時教使然也。

translations.literal: Yet originally none were descendants of dukes or marquises, none were clerks of senior officials, none had the force of a foot or an inch of territory; they rose from lanes and alleys, leaned on thorn staffs and spear-shafts, moved in response to the times, rose together without planning, assembled without covenant, extended territory and advanced land, reaching to hegemon-kings—it was the teaching of the times that made it so.
translations.idiomatic: Yet none of these men sprang from ducal houses or high office; they held not an inch of legitimate power. They came out of the back alleys with improvised weapons, stirred by the moment, acting without prior conspiracy yet rising as one, seizing ground until some stood as kings or hegemons—such was the lesson the age had taught them.

sentences.zh: 秦貴為天子，富有天下，滅世絕祀，窮兵之禍也。

translations.literal: Qin was honored as Son of Heaven and rich with the realm; its generations were extinguished and sacrifices cut off—the disaster of exhausted arms.
translations.idiomatic: Though Qin had worn the imperial crown and owned the wealth of the world, its line was extinguished and its ancestral rites cut short—this is what endless war buys a dynasty.

sentences.zh: 故周失之弱，秦失之彊，不變之患也。

translations.literal: Therefore Zhou lost through weakness, Qin lost through strength—the misfortune of not changing.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou fell because it grew too weak to act; Qin fell because it clung too fiercely to force. Both perished from the same refusal to change course.

sentences.zh: 今徇南夷，朝夜郎，降羌僰，略薉州，建城邑，深入匈奴，燔其龍城，議者美之。

translations.literal: Now you display the southern Yi, have Yelang court, reduce Qiang and Bo, seize the foul region, build cities and towns, deeply enter the Xiongnu, burn their Dragon City—discussers praise it.
translations.idiomatic: Today the court campaigns in the southern tribes, brings Yelang to audience, subdues Qiang and Bo peoples, seizes border prefectures, founds new towns, drives deep into the steppe, and burns the Xiongnu court at Longcheng—and advisers call all of it glorious.

sentences.zh: 此人臣之利，非天下之長策也。

translations.literal: This is benefit to ministers, not the long plan for the realm.
translations.idiomatic: That may serve ambitious officials; it is no lasting policy for the empire.

sentences.zh: 今中國無狗吠之警，而外累於遠方之備，靡敝國家，非所以子民也。

translations.literal: Now the central states have no alarm of dogs barking, yet outwardly are burdened with preparations in distant places, wasting and exhausting the state—this is not the way to treat the people as children.
translations.idiomatic: The heartland faces no threat that would set village dogs barking, yet we strain the treasury on garrisons thousands of miles away. Bleeding the state dry is no way to care for the people as a father should.

sentences.zh: 行無窮之欲，甘心快意，結怨於匈奴，非所以安邊也。

translations.literal: Pursuing desires without end, gratifying the heart with pleasure, tying resentment with the Xiongnu—this is not the way to secure the border.
translations.idiomatic: To indulge boundless appetite for glory, savor short-term triumph, and pile up lasting hatred with the Xiongnu is no recipe for a quiet frontier.

sentences.zh: 禍挐而不解，兵休而復起，近者愁苦，遠者驚駭，非所以持久也。

translations.literal: Disaster grasps and does not loosen; troops rest and rise again; those near are anxious and bitter, those far are frightened—this is not the way to endure long.
translations.idiomatic: Calamity clings without release; each pause in fighting is followed by a new mobilization. Those nearby groan under the burden; distant peoples tremble at the rumor of arms. No dynasty can last on such a footing.

sentences.zh: 今天下鍛甲摩劍，矯箭控弦，轉輸軍糧，未見休時，此天下所共憂也。

translations.literal: Now the realm hammers armor and polishes swords, straightens arrows and draws bowstrings, transports and conveys military grain—no time of rest is seen; this is what the realm jointly worries about.
translations.idiomatic: Across the empire men beat mail, hone blades, straighten shafts, and string bows, while grain trains roll east and west without cease. This is the specter that haunts every household.

sentences.zh: 夫兵久而變起，事煩而慮生。

translations.literal: When arms are long, changes arise; when affairs are numerous, cares are born.
translations.idiomatic: Protracted war breeds mutiny; tangled administration breeds fresh crises.

sentences.zh: 今外郡之地或幾千里，列城數十，形束壤制，帶脅諸侯，非宗室之利也。

translations.literal: Now the lands of outer commanderies are sometimes nearly a thousand li, arrayed cities are several tens; form binds and soil restrains, girdling and threatening the feudal lords—it is not benefit to the imperial house.
translations.idiomatic: Some frontier commanderies sprawl across a thousand li and hold dozens of walled towns whose geography can choke rival powers as a belt tightens the waist. That strength lies outside the imperial clan, and it bodes ill for the throne.

sentences.zh: 上觀齊晉所以亡，公室卑削，六卿大盛也；

translations.literal: Looking up, observe how Qi and Jin perished—the ducal house was humble and cut down, the six ministers greatly flourished.
translations.idiomatic: Consider why Qi and Jin fell: their ruling houses withered while great ministerial clans grew too strong to curb.

sentences.zh: 下覽秦之所以滅，刑嚴文刻，欲大無窮也。

translations.literal: Looking down, survey why Qin was destroyed—punishments were harsh and writing was carved, desires were great without end.
translations.idiomatic: Look lower, at Qin's ruin: savage law, pitiless edicts, and ambition without limit.

sentences.zh: 今郡守之權非特六卿之重也，地幾千里非特閭巷之資也，甲兵器械非特棘矜之用也，以逢萬世之變，則不可勝諱也。

translations.literal: Now the power of a commandery governor is not merely the weight of the six ministers; land of nearly a thousand li is not merely the capital of lanes and alleys; armor, weapons, and implements are not merely the use of thorn staffs and spear-shafts—if one meets the changes of ten thousand generations, then it cannot be fully concealed.
translations.idiomatic: A modern governor commands more real authority than any of Jin's six ministers; his domain is vaster than a rebel's alley-kingdom; his arsenals hold more than thornwood spears. Should the world shift again, the danger is too obvious to gloss over.

sentences.zh: 後以安為騎馬令。

translations.literal: Later, because of this, An was made Master of Horse.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward Yan An was appointed Master of the Imperial Horse.

sentences.zh: 終軍字子雲，濟南人也。

translations.literal: Zhong Jun, courtesy name Ziyun, was a man of Jinan.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Jun, whose courtesy name was Ziyun, came from Jinan.

sentences.zh: 少好學，以辯博能屬文聞於郡中。

translations.literal: In youth he loved learning; by eloquence, breadth, and ability to compose texts he was heard of in the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: From boyhood he loved study and won countywide renown for wide reading, sharp debate, and a ready pen.

sentences.zh: 年十八，選為博士弟子。

translations.literal: At age eighteen, he was selected as a disciple of the erudites.
translations.idiomatic: At eighteen he was chosen as a student of the imperial academy.

sentences.zh: 至府受遣，太守聞其有異材，召見軍，甚奇之，與交結。

translations.literal: When he arrived at the office to receive dispatch, the grand administrator heard that he had unusual talent, summoned and met Jun, greatly marveled at him, and formed ties with him.
translations.idiomatic: When he went to the yamen for his travel orders, the grand administrator heard reports of his gifts, called him in, was deeply impressed, and struck up a friendship.

sentences.zh: 軍揖太守而去，至長安上書言事。

translations.literal: Jun bowed to the grand administrator and departed; he reached Chang'an and submitted a memorial discussing affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Jun bowed once to the governor and left for the capital, where he filed a memorial on state policy.

sentences.zh: 武帝異其文，拜軍為謁者給事中。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu marveled at his writing and appointed Jun Herald and Palace Attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu was struck by the quality of his prose and named him court herald with standing access to the inner palace.

sentences.zh: 從上幸雍祠五畤，獲白麟，一角而五蹄。

translations.literal: He followed the emperor in visiting Yong to sacrifice at the five altars; they obtained a white unicorn, one horn and five hoofs.
translations.idiomatic: He accompanied the sovereign to Yong for the sacrifices at the five shrines, where hunters brought in a white unicorn bearing a single horn and five hooves.

sentences.zh: 時又得奇木，其枝旁出，輒復合於木上。

translations.literal: At the same time they also obtained a strange tree; its branches came out sideways and then again joined together on the tree.
translations.idiomatic: About the same time someone presented a freakish tree whose limbs jutted sideways only to fuse back into the trunk.

sentences.zh: 上異此二物，博謀群臣。

translations.literal: The emperor marveled at these two things and broadly consulted the ministers.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor regarded both prodigies as extraordinary and asked his officials for wide-ranging counsel.

sentences.zh: 軍上對曰：

translations.literal: Jun submitted a reply, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Jun responded with a memorial that read:

sentences.zh: 臣聞詩頌君德，樂舞后功，異經而同指，明盛德之所隆也。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that the Odes praise the lord's virtue, music and dance extol the later achievement; different classics but the same aim, clarifying where abundant virtue rises.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Odes hymns a ruler's virtue; ritual music celebrates deeds handed down from sage-kings. The texts differ, yet they point the same way: they mark where true greatness gathers.

sentences.zh: 南越竄屏葭葦，與鳥魚群，正朔不及其俗。

translations.literal: Southern Yue hides among reeds and rushes, flocks with birds and fish; the correct beginning of the year does not reach their customs.
translations.idiomatic: The Yue huddle beyond the reeds like waterfowl and fish; our calendar and rites have never touched their ways.

sentences.zh: 有司臨境，而東甌內附，閩王伏辜，南越賴救。

translations.literal: When officials approached the border, Eastern Ou submitted within, the king of Min submitted to guilt, and Southern Yue relied on rescue.
translations.idiomatic: As soon as imperial officers reached the frontier, Eastern Ou came in, the king of Min accepted punishment, and Yue itself owed its survival to Han mercy.

sentences.zh: 北胡隨畜薦居，禽獸行，虎狼心，上古未能攝。

translations.literal: The northern Hu follow livestock and shift dwelling; they act like birds and beasts, have hearts like tigers and wolves; high antiquity could not subdue them.
translations.idiomatic: The northern nomads drift with their herds like animals, with appetites as fierce as wolves'. Even the sage-kings of high antiquity could not fully tame them.

sentences.zh: 大將軍秉鉞，單于奔幕；

translations.literal: The great general grasped the axe; the Chanyu fled to the desert curtain.
translations.idiomatic: The supreme commander took the war-axe in hand and drove the Chanyu headlong into the steppe.

sentences.zh: 票騎抗旌，昆邪右衽。

translations.literal: The Piaoqi general raised the banner; Kunye wore the right lapel.
translations.idiomatic: The Swift Cavalry general unfurled his standard until Prince Hunye surrendered and adopted Han dress.

sentences.zh: 是澤南洽而威北暢也。

translations.literal: This is grace south reaching and might north extending.
translations.idiomatic: Thus kindness has soaked the south while awe runs clear across the north.

sentences.zh: 若罰不阿近，舉不遺遠，設官俟賢，縣賞待功，能者進以保祿，罷者退而勞力，刑於宇內矣。

translations.literal: If punishments do not favor the near, promotions do not omit the far, offices are set up awaiting the worthy, rewards are hung awaiting merit, the able advance to preserve salary, the incompetent retire to labor with strength—then there is model within the cosmos.
translations.idiomatic: When penalties spare no favorite, promotions reach the humblest talent, posts await true worth, honors hang ready for merit, capable men rise to secure their stipends while the inept step down to manual work, the whole realm becomes your mirror of justice.

sentences.zh: 履眾美而不足，懷聖明而不專，建三宮之文質，章厥職之所宜，封禪之君無聞焉。

translations.literal: Treading many beauties yet not sufficing, embracing sagely brightness yet not monopolizing, establishing the pattern and substance of the three palaces, clarifying what suits each duty—of lords who performed feng and shan, none are heard of thus.
translations.idiomatic: You walk amid every excellence yet still feel wanting; you hold sagely insight yet refuse to monopolize it; you set the three palaces in proper balance of ornament and plainness and define each office's charge. No ruler who merely mounted Mount Tai for feng and shan rites ever matched that.

sentences.zh: 夫人命初定，萬事草創，及臻六合同風，九州共貫，必待明聖潤色，祖業傳於無窮。

translations.literal: When the mandate of man is first fixed, the myriad things are begun; when it reaches the six directions sharing one wind and the nine provinces sharing one strand, one must await a bright sage to polish, and the ancestral enterprise is transmitted to infinity.
translations.idiomatic: At the founding of a house all institutions are rough; only when the six quarters breathe a single culture and the nine regions run on one thread does a truly enlightened sovereign put the finishing polish on them, so the patrimony may pass down without end.

sentences.zh: 故周至成王，然後制定，而休徵之應見。

translations.literal: Therefore Zhou reached King Cheng, and afterward regulations were fixed, and the response of auspicious signs appeared.
translations.idiomatic: The house of Zhou did not complete its statutes until the reign of King Cheng; only then did propitious omens begin to answer its virtue.

sentences.zh: 陛下盛日月之光，垂聖思於勒成，專神明之敬，奉燔瘞於郊宮，獻享之精交神，積和之氣塞明，而異獸來獲，宜矣。

translations.literal: Your Majesty flourishes the light of sun and moon, hangs sagely thought on carved completion, devotes reverence to spirits, offers burning and burying at the suburban palace; the essence of offerings and feasting intersects spirits, accumulated harmony qi fills brightness, and strange beasts come to be captured—it is fitting.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty outshines sun and moon, bends sage reflection toward perfecting the record, concentrates reverence on the gods, and presents burnt and buried offerings at the suburban shrines until the subtle ether of sacrifice mingles with the spirits and a tide of harmony fills the bright sky. Small wonder strange beasts should present themselves.

sentences.zh: 昔武王中流未濟，白魚入於王舟，俯取以燎，群公咸曰「休哉！」

translations.literal: Formerly King Wu, midstream not yet crossed, a white fish entered the king's boat; he bent and took it to burn as offering; the dukes all said, 'Auspicious!'
translations.idiomatic: Once, when King Wu had not yet finished fording the river, a white fish leaped into his barge; he lifted it out for a burnt offering, and the nobles cried, 'Excellent omen!'

sentences.zh: 今郊祀未見於神祇，而獲獸以饋，此天之所以示饗，而上通之符合也。

translations.literal: Now suburban sacrifice has not yet been seen by the spirits, yet a beast was obtained to present as offering—this is what Heaven uses to show feasting, and the tally of communication on high.
translations.idiomatic: You have not yet announced the suburban rites to the high gods, yet Heaven sends a beast fit for the altar—clear proof that the spirits accept your worship and that your virtue resonates above.

sentences.zh: 宜因昭時令日，改定告元，苴以白茅於江淮，發嘉號于營丘，以應緝熙，使著事者有紀焉。

translations.literal: It is fitting to follow the bright season and auspicious day, change and fix the proclamation of the first year, wrap with white rushes at the Yangzi and Huai, issue a fine title at Yingqiu, in order to respond to bright progress, so that those who record affairs have a record.
translations.idiomatic: Seize an auspicious season and day to proclaim a new era name, invest the princes along the Yangzi and Huai with bundles of white rushes, and broadcast a glorious title from Yingqiu, echoing the bright mandate so historians may set it down in good order.

sentences.zh: 蓋六鶂退飛，逆也；

translations.literal: As for six wild geese flying backward—it is contrary.
translations.idiomatic: When six wild geese beat their way upwind, the Spring and Autumn Annals read it as ill omen.

sentences.zh: 白魚登舟，順也。

translations.literal: The white fish ascending the boat is compliant.
translations.idiomatic: The white fish leaping into the royal boat betokened favor from Heaven.

sentences.zh: 夫明闇之徵，上亂飛鳥，下動淵魚，各以類推。

translations.literal: As for the signs of brightness and darkness, above they disturb flying birds, below they move deep fish—each is inferred by category.
translations.idiomatic: Omens of light and shadow work by kind: Heaven stirs the birds aloft, and the fish below feel it too.

sentences.zh: 今野獸并角，明同本也；

translations.literal: Now wild beasts share horns—brightness is the same root.
translations.idiomatic: A beast in the wild with twin horns merged as one proclaims that all creatures share a single root.

sentences.zh: 眾支內附，示無外也。

translations.literal: Many branches attach within—showing there is nothing outside.
translations.idiomatic: When every branch people turns inward to the throne, it signals that nothing lies beyond the emperor's embrace.

sentences.zh: 若此之應，殆將有解編髮，削左衽，襲冠帶，要衣裳，而蒙化者焉。

translations.literal: Responses like this—perhaps there will be those who undo braided hair, cut the left lapel, put on cap and belt, gird robe and skirt, and receive transformation.
translations.idiomatic: Portents such as these foretell tribes who will unbind their hair, cut away barbarian dress, and don caps, sashes, robes, and skirts to receive your civilization.

sentences.zh: 斯拱而俟之耳！

translations.literal: This is folding hands and awaiting it!
translations.idiomatic: You have only to fold your hands and wait.

sentences.zh: 對奏，上甚異之，由是改元為元狩。

translations.literal: When the reply was presented, the emperor greatly marveled at it; thereby the era name was changed to Yuanshou.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was so struck by the memorial that he renamed the reign Yuanshou.

sentences.zh: 後數月，越地及匈奴名王有率眾來降者，時皆以軍言為中。

translations.literal: Several months later, in Yue territory and among the Xiongnu famous kings there were those who led crowds to come surrender; at the time all took Jun's words as hitting the mark.
translations.idiomatic: Within a few months Yue chiefs and noted Xiongnu princes began leading their followers in to surrender—just as Zhong Jun had predicted.

sentences.zh: 元鼎中，博士徐偃使行風俗。

translations.literal: In the Yuanding period, Erudite Xu Yan was sent to tour customs.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yuanding era the court scholar Xu Yan was dispatched to inspect local customs.

sentences.zh: 偃矯制，使膠東、魯國鼓鑄鹽鐵。

translations.literal: Yan forged an edict, causing Jiaodong and the state of Lu to smelt and cast salt and iron.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Yan forged imperial orders and had Jiaodong and Lu cast iron and boil salt on their own.

sentences.zh: 還，奏事，徙為太常丞。

translations.literal: Returning, he reported affairs and was transferred to be assistant to the Chamberlain for Ceremonials.
translations.idiomatic: On his return he filed his report and was promoted to assistant director under the Chamberlain for Ceremonials.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫張湯劾偃矯制大害，法至死。

translations.literal: Imperial Counselor Zhang Tang impeached Yan for forging an edict with great harm; by law it reached death.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial Counselor Zhang Tang impeached him for counterfeiting orders with grave consequences—a capital crime.

sentences.zh: 偃以為春秋之義，大夫出疆，有可以安社稷，存萬民，顓之可也。

translations.literal: Yan thought that according to the meaning of the Spring and Autumn Annals, when a grandee goes beyond the border, if there is something by which he can secure the altars of soil and grain and preserve the myriad people, to assume authority is permissible.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Yan cited the Spring and Autumn principle that a minister abroad may act on his own authority if the altars and the lives of the people hang in the balance.

sentences.zh: 湯以致其法，不能詘其義。

translations.literal: Tang by this applied the law but could not refute his meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Tang could press the statute but could not overturn the moral argument.

sentences.zh: 有詔下軍問狀，軍詰偃曰：「古者諸侯國異俗分，百里不通，時有聘會之事，安危之勢，呼吸成變，故有不受辭造命顓己之宜；

translations.literal: There was an edict sent down to Jun to ask the circumstances; Jun interrogated Yan, saying: 'In antiquity the feudal states differed in custom and were divided; within a hundred li they did not communicate; from time to time there were matters of visiting and meeting; the posture of safety and danger could change in a breath; therefore there was propriety in not receiving orders and making commands, assuming authority for oneself.'
translations.idiomatic: An edict ordered Zhong Jun to examine the case. He questioned Xu Yan: 'In ancient times each feudal state kept its own ways; a hundred li might as well have been a world apart. Envoys hurried between courts because fortune could turn in a breath, so a minister might rightly assume emergency powers without waiting for a written order.'

sentences.zh: 今天下為一，萬里同風，故春秋『王者無外』。

translations.literal: Now the realm is one, ten thousand li share one wind; therefore the Spring and Autumn Annals say "the king has nothing outside."
translations.idiomatic: Today the empire is one house under a single wind for ten thousand li, and the Annals teach that the king knows no "outside."

sentences.zh: 偃巡封域之中，稱以出疆何也？

translations.literal: Yan toured within the sealed domain—calling it going beyond the border, how is that?
translations.idiomatic: You never left the emperor's domain—how can you call that "crossing the border"?

sentences.zh: 且鹽鐵，郡有餘臧，正二國廢，國家不足以為利害，而以安社稷存萬民為辭，何也？」

translations.literal: Moreover as for salt and iron, the commanderies have surplus in store; whether or not the two kingdoms' monopolies were suspended would not materially help or harm the central government—so to cite securing the altars and preserving the myriad people as your excuse, how is that?
translations.idiomatic: Besides, every commandery stockpiles salt and iron; ending the two kingdoms' local monopolies would barely touch the central treasury. What sense is there in pleading the fate of the altars and the people?

sentences.zh: 又詰偃：「膠東南近琅邪，北接北海，魯國西枕泰山，東有東海，受其鹽鐵。

translations.literal: He again interrogated Yan: 'Jiaodong on the south nears Langye, on the north joins Beihai; the state of Lu on the west pillows Mount Tai, on the east has the Eastern Sea—they draw on their own salt and iron.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Jun pressed further: 'Jiaodong borders Langye to the south and Beihai to the north; Lu lies against Mount Tai in the west and the Eastern Sea in the east—both regions already draw salt and iron from their own ground.'

sentences.zh: 偃度四郡口數田地，率其用器食鹽，不足以并給二郡邪？

translations.literal: Yan estimated the four commanderies' population numbers and fields and lands; generally their tools and eating salt—was it not enough to supply together the two states?
translations.idiomatic: If you reckoned the population and acreage of the four neighboring commanderies, could their usual tools and salt rations not have supplied both kingdoms?

sentences.zh: 將勢宜有餘，而吏不能也？

translations.literal: Or was the situation fitting to have surplus, yet the clerks could not?
translations.idiomatic: Or was there plenty of capacity while the local officials simply failed to manage it?

sentences.zh: 何以言之？

translations.literal: By what is this said?
translations.idiomatic: What proves that?

sentences.zh: 偃矯制而鼓鑄者，欲及春耕種贍民器也。

translations.literal: Yan forged an edict and smelted and cast because he wished to catch up with spring plowing and planting to supply the people's tools.
translations.idiomatic: You forged orders to cast tools, you say, so peasants would have iron for the spring planting.

sentences.zh: 今魯國之鼓，當先具其備，至秋乃能舉火。

translations.literal: Now as for Lu's smelting, one must first complete its preparations; only by autumn can fires be raised.
translations.idiomatic: If Lu were really to fire its forges, the works would need to be readied first; the furnaces could not even be lit until autumn.

sentences.zh: 此言與實反者非？

translations.literal: Is this not words and facts being opposite?
translations.idiomatic: Does that not flatly contradict what you claim?

sentences.zh: 偃已前三奏，無詔，不惟所為不許，而直矯作威福，以從民望，干名采譽，此明聖所必加誅也。

translations.literal: Yan had already thrice submitted memorials earlier, without edict; not only were his actions not permitted, but he directly forged authority, making awe and blessings, in order to follow the people's hopes, seeking name and harvesting reputation—this is what a clear sage must add execution to.
translations.idiomatic: You had already memorialized three times and received no imperial reply. Not only were you never authorized to act, you forged orders, threw your weight about, pandered to popular opinion, and traded on moral prestige for personal fame—conduct that any enlightened ruler would punish with death.

sentences.zh: 『枉尺直尋』，孟子稱其不可；

translations.literal: 'Bending a foot to straighten eight feet'—Mencius called it unacceptable;
translations.idiomatic: Mencius rejected the notion of bending a cubit to gain eight cubits;

sentences.zh: 今所犯罪重，所就者小，偃自予必死而為之邪？

translations.literal: Now the crime committed is heavy, what was accomplished is small—did you yourself grant that you would surely die and still do it?
translations.idiomatic: Your offense is grave and your supposed public benefit slight. Did you expect execution and press ahead anyway?

sentences.zh: 將幸誅不加，欲以采名也？」

translations.literal: Or did you hope punishment would not be applied and wished to harvest reputation?
translations.idiomatic: Or did you count on escaping the law and merely wanted the glory?

sentences.zh: 偃窮詘，服罪當死。

translations.literal: Yan was exhausted and refuted; he admitted guilt fitting death.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Yan had no answer left; he confessed and accepted that he deserved death.

sentences.zh: 軍奏「偃矯制顓行，非奉使體，請下御史徵偃即罪。」

translations.literal: Jun memorialized: 'Yan forged edicts and acted on his own authority; it is not in the form of one who bears a mission; request to send down to the Imperial Clerk to summon Yan to answer for his crime.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Jun reported that Xu Yan had counterfeited orders and acted arbitrarily—conduct unworthy of an imperial envoy—and asked that the case be handed to the censor so Xu Yan could be arrested and sentenced.

sentences.zh: 奏可。

translations.literal: The memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The throne approved the memorial.

sentences.zh: 上善其詰，有詔示御史大夫。

translations.literal: The emperor praised his examination; there was an edict shown to the Imperial Counselor.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor commended Zhong Jun's cross-examination and issued an edict laying out the case for the Imperial Counselor.

sentences.zh: 初，軍從濟南當詣博士，步入關，關吏予軍繻。

translations.literal: At first, when Jun was going from Jinan to attend the erudites, he walked into the pass; the pass clerk gave Jun a silk tally.
translations.idiomatic: Long ago, when Zhong Jun left Jinan to enroll as a student of the imperial academy, he entered the frontier pass on foot, and the gate officer handed him the return-token of split silk.

sentences.zh: 軍問：「以此何為？」

translations.literal: Jun asked: 'By this, what is to be done?'
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Jun asked, 'What is this for?'

sentences.zh: 吏曰；

translations.literal: The clerk said;
translations.idiomatic: The officer replied,

sentences.zh: 「為復傳，還當以合符。」

translations.literal: 'It is for the return pass; when returning you must match the tally.'
translations.idiomatic: 'It is your return chit; when you come back you must present the matching half.'

sentences.zh: 軍曰：「大丈夫西游，終不復傳還。」

translations.literal: Jun said: 'A great man travels west—will never use the return pass to come back.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Jun said, 'A man of spirit who sets out for the west does not plan to slink home on a return pass.'

sentences.zh: 棄繻而去。

translations.literal: He discarded the silk and departed.
translations.idiomatic: He threw away the token and walked on.

sentences.zh: 軍為謁者，使行郡國，建節東出關，關吏識之，曰：「此使者乃前棄繻生也。」

translations.literal: When Jun became Herald, sent to tour commanderies and kingdoms, bearing the scepter he went east out of the pass; the pass clerk recognized him and said: 'This envoy is that former youth who discarded the silk.'
translations.idiomatic: Later, as court herald on an inspection tour of the commanderies, Zhong Jun rode east through the same pass with the imperial credentia staff. The gate officer recognized him and exclaimed, 'This is the young scholar who threw away his chit!'

sentences.zh: 軍行郡國，所見便宜以聞。

translations.literal: When Jun toured commanderies and kingdoms, whatever he saw that was advantageous he reported.
translations.idiomatic: Wherever he went, he reported every useful measure or abuse he observed.

sentences.zh: 還奏事，上甚說。

translations.literal: Returning, he reported affairs; the emperor was very pleased.
translations.idiomatic: When he returned to give account, the emperor was delighted.

sentences.zh: 當發使使匈奴，軍自請曰：「軍無橫草之功，得列宿衛，食祿五年。

translations.literal: When it was time to dispatch envoys to the Xiongnu, Jun volunteered, saying: 'Jun has no merit of laying grass across (the path), yet was able to be ranked among the palace guard, receiving salary five years.'
translations.idiomatic: When envoys were to be sent to the Xiongnu, Zhong Jun volunteered: 'I have never so much as thrown a blade of grass across the battlefield for the dynasty, yet I have stood in the palace guard and drawn a stipend for five years.'

sentences.zh: 邊境時有風塵之警，臣宜被堅執銳，當矢石，啟前行。

translations.literal: The border from time to time has alarms of wind and dust; your servant ought to don hard armor and grasp sharp weapons, stand against arrows and stones, open the forward march.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the frontier flares with war, a subject ought to buckle on armor, take up arms, face arrow and stone, and lead the van.

sentences.zh: 駑下不習金革之事，今聞將遣匈奴使者，臣願盡精厲氣，奉佐明使，畫吉凶於單于之前。

translations.literal: Your servant is lowly and does not practice metal-and-leather affairs; now hearing that envoys will be sent to the Xiongnu, your servant wishes to exhaust essence and sharpen spirit, to assist the enlightened envoy, and paint good and ill before the Chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: I am a poor hand at war. Now that the court will send an embassy to the Chanyu, I beg to marshal all my wit and zeal as aide to the chief envoy and to lay out before the Chanyu himself where fortune and peril lie.

sentences.zh: 臣年少材下，孤於外官，不足以亢一方之任，竊不勝憤懣。」

translations.literal: Your servant is young and talent is low, isolated in outer offices, not sufficient to bear one region's responsibility—privately cannot overcome frustration.'
translations.idiomatic: I am young, my talents slight, and I have languished in minor posts without a frontier command worthy of the trust you place in me—the thought leaves me choked with frustration.

sentences.zh: 詔問畫吉凶之狀，上奇軍對，擢為諫大夫。

translations.literal: An edict asked about the situation of painting good and ill; the emperor marveled at Jun's reply and promoted him to Grandee Remonstrant.
translations.idiomatic: An edict asked him to spell out what he meant by laying out good and ill fortune. The emperor was so struck by his answer that he promoted him to Grandee Remonstrant.

sentences.zh: 南越與漢和親，乃遣軍使南越，說其王，欲令入朝，比內諸侯。

translations.literal: Southern Yue made peace and kinship with Han; then they sent Jun as envoy to Southern Yue, persuading its king, wishing to make him enter court, comparable to inner feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: After Southern Yue sealed a marriage alliance with Han, the court dispatched Zhong Jun to persuade its king to come to audience and accept the status of an internal feudatory.

sentences.zh: 軍自請：「願受長纓，必羈南越王而致之闕下。」

translations.literal: Jun himself requested: 'I wish to receive a long cord—I will surely tie the King of Southern Yue and bring him beneath the palace gate.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Jun volunteered: 'Give me a long cord and I will bind the King of Southern Yue and haul him to the palace steps.'

sentences.zh: 軍遂往說越王，越王聽許，請舉國內屬。

translations.literal: Jun then went to persuade the Yue king; the Yue king listened and assented, requesting to offer the whole state in internal submission.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Jun went south and won the king's ear; the king agreed to place his entire realm under Han.

sentences.zh: 天子大說，賜南越大臣印綬，壹用漢法，以新改其俗，令使者留填撫之。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven was greatly pleased, granted Southern Yue great ministers seals and ribbons, uniformly applied Han law, in order to renew and change their customs, ordered envoys to remain to fill and pacify.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was overjoyed. He issued Han seals and ribbons to Yue ministers, extended Han law uniformly to refashion local custom, and ordered the envoys to stay behind to stabilize the new order.

sentences.zh: 越相呂嘉不欲內屬，發兵攻殺其王，及漢使者皆死。

translations.literal: The Yue chancellor Lü Jia did not wish internal submission; he raised troops, attacked and killed the king, and the Han envoys all died.
translations.idiomatic: The Yue prime minister Lü Jia refused submission, rose in arms, murdered his king, and killed the Han envoys as well.

sentences.zh: 語在南越傳。

translations.literal: The words are in the Biography of Southern Yue.
translations.idiomatic: The full story is told in the biography of Southern Yue.

sentences.zh: 軍死時年二十餘，故世謂之「終童」。

translations.literal: When Jun died he was over twenty; therefore the world calls him 'Zhong the Boy.'
translations.idiomatic: He was barely past twenty when he died, and posterity remembers him as 'Zhong the lad'—the boy who never grew old.

sentences.zh: 王褒字子淵，蜀人也。

translations.literal: Wang Bao, courtesy name Ziyuan, was a man of Shu.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Bao, courtesy name Ziyuan, was a native of Shu.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時修武帝故事，講論六藝群書，博盡奇異之好，徵能為楚辭九江被公，召見誦讀，益召高材劉向、張子僑、華龍、柳褒等待詔金馬門。

translations.literal: In Emperor Xuan's time they restored Emperor Wu's precedents, lectured and discussed the six arts and collected books, broadly exhausting the fondness for strange things; they summoned one who could compose Chu Ci—Bei Gong of Jiujiang—summoned and saw him recite; further summoned talented men Liu Xiang, Zhang Ziqiao, Hua Long, Liu Bao, and others to await edict at the Golden Horse Gate.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan the court revived Emperor Wu's cultural program: debate on the Six Classics and the wider corpus, a restless taste for the rare and marvelous. It summoned Bei Gong of Jiujiang, famed for his Chu-style verse, heard him recite, and then added brilliant men such as Liu Xiang, Zhang Ziqiao, Hua Long, and Liu Bao to the roster of scholars awaiting summons at the Golden Horse Gate.

sentences.zh: 神爵、五鳳之間，天下殷當，數有嘉應。

translations.literal: Between the Shenjue and Wufeng periods, the realm was abundant and appropriate; repeatedly there were auspicious responses.
translations.idiomatic: Between the Shenjue and Wufeng reign titles the empire prospered, and auspicious omens arrived again and again.

sentences.zh: 上頗作歌詩，欲興協律之事，丞相魏相奏言知音善鼓雅琴者渤海趙定、梁國龔德，皆召見待詔。

translations.literal: The emperor somewhat composed songs and poems, wishing to raise the business of harmonizing pitch; Chancellor Wei Xiang memorialized saying those who knew tones and were good at strumming the ya zither—Zhao Ding of Bohai and Gong De of Liang—were all summoned and awaited edict.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor himself began writing lyrics and wished to revive the office for tuning pitch standards. Chancellor Wei Xiang recommended Zhao Ding of Bohai and Gong De of Liang, both masters of the classical zither, and they too were summoned to the waiting roster.

sentences.zh: 於是益州刺史王襄欲宣風化於眾庶，聞王褒有俊材，請與相見，使褒作中和、樂職、宣布詩，選好事者令依鹿鳴之聲習而歌之。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Governor of Yizhou, Wang Xiang, wished to spread transformation by custom among the multitude; hearing that Wang Bao had outstanding talent, he requested to meet him, had Bao compose the poems 'Central Harmony,' 'Joy in Office,' and 'Proclamation,' selected those fond of affairs and ordered them to practice and sing according to the sound of 'Deer Call.'
translations.idiomatic: The governor of Yizhou, Wang Xiang, hoped to bring moral suasion to the common people. Learning of Wang Bao's gifts, he invited him in and commissioned the poems 'Central Harmony,' 'Joy in Duty,' and 'The Proclamation,' then picked enthusiasts to set them to the tune of the 'Deer Call' ode and teach them by singing.

sentences.zh: 時氾鄉侯何武為僮子，選在歌中。

translations.literal: At that time Marquis of Fanxiang He Wu was a boy; he was selected to be in the songs.
translations.idiomatic: Among the choristers was the future Marquis of Fanxiang, He Wu, still a schoolboy.

sentences.zh: 久之，武等學長安，歌太學下，轉而上聞。

translations.literal: After a long time, Wu and others studied in Chang'an, sang beneath the Imperial Academy, and in turn it was heard above.
translations.idiomatic: Years later, when He Wu and his companions were students in Chang'an, they sang the pieces below the walls of the Imperial Academy until word of them reached the throne.

sentences.zh: 宣帝召見武等觀之，皆賜帛，謂曰：「此盛德之事，吾何足以當之！」

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan summoned Wu and others to observe them; all were granted silk; he said to them: 'This is a matter of abundant virtue—how am I sufficient to match it!'
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan called He Wu and his fellows in to perform, rewarded each with silk, and said, 'Such music belongs to a reign of supreme virtue; how can I deserve it?'

sentences.zh: 褒既為刺史作頌，又作其傳，益州刺史因奏褒有軼材。

translations.literal: Bao, having composed a eulogy for the governor, also composed his biography; the Governor of Yizhou thereupon memorialized that Bao had outstanding talent.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Bao had not only written the governor's celebratory verse but drafted his biography as well, and on that basis the governor reported Wang Bao's exceptional gifts to the capital.

sentences.zh: 上乃徵褒。

translations.literal: The emperor then summoned Bao.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor thereupon summoned Wang Bao to court.

sentences.zh: 既至，詔褎為聖主得賢臣頌其意。

translations.literal: When he had arrived, an edict ordered Bao to eulogize the meaning of 'The Enlightened Lord Obtains Worthy Ministers.'
translations.idiomatic: Once he arrived, an edict instructed him to compose a rhapsody expounding the theme 'How a sage ruler wins worthy ministers.'

sentences.zh: 褒對曰：

translations.literal: Bao replied, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Wang Bao replied:

sentences.zh: 夫荷旃被毳者，難與道純綿之麗密；

translations.literal: Those who bear felt and wear coarse wool—it is hard with them to speak of pure silk's beautiful density;
translations.idiomatic: A man bundled in felt and coarse fur will not appreciate the fine weave of pure silk;

sentences.zh: 羹黎唅糗者，不足與論太牢之滋味。

translations.literal: Those who sup on coarse grain and hold dry rations in the mouth are not sufficient to discuss the great sacrifice's flavors.
translations.idiomatic: one who lives on millet gruel and dry rations is no judge of the taste of a full royal banquet.

sentences.zh: 今臣辟在西蜀，生於窮巷之中，長於蓬茨之下，無有游觀廣覽之知，顧有至愚極陋之累，不足以塞厚望，應明指。

translations.literal: Now your servant is rustic in western Shu, born in exhausted lanes, grown up beneath tumbleweed and thatch; I have no knowledge of roaming observation and broad viewing; I only have the burden of utmost stupidity and extreme coarseness—not sufficient to fill high hopes or respond to clear directions.
translations.idiomatic: I am a rustic from the far west, bred in a back alley and raised under a thatched roof. I have never traveled to broaden my mind; I bear instead the heaviest load of ignorance. I cannot satisfy your lofty expectations or meet the clarity of your command.

sentences.zh: 雖然，敢不略陳愚而抒情素！

translations.literal: Even so, how dare I not briefly state my stupidity and express plain feeling!
translations.idiomatic: Yet I would be craven not to sketch my humble thoughts and lay bare my heart.

sentences.zh: 記曰：共惟春秋五始之要，在乎審己正統而已。

translations.literal: The record says: reverently consider the Spring and Autumn Annals' five beginnings' essentials—they lie in examining oneself and rectifying the lineage, that is all.
translations.idiomatic: The classic says that the crux of the Annals' doctrine of the five beginnings is simply to know oneself and set the true succession straight.

sentences.zh: 夫賢者，國家之器用也。

translations.literal: As for the worthy, they are the state's tools and implements.
translations.idiomatic: Worthy men are the instruments of state.

sentences.zh: 所任賢，則趨舍省而功施普；

translations.literal: If those employed are worthy, then rushing and abandoning are reduced and merit spreads broadly;
translations.idiomatic: Employ the right men and effort is spared while their good work reaches everywhere;

sentences.zh: 器用利，則用力少而就效眾。

translations.literal: if tools and implements are sharp, then labor used is little yet completed effects are many.
translations.idiomatic: give them sharp tools and little labor yields great results.

sentences.zh: 故工人之用鈍器也，勞筋苦骨，終日矻矻。

translations.literal: Therefore when craftsmen use blunt implements, sinews labor and bones suffer, all day toiling.
translations.idiomatic: A craftsman with a dull blade wears himself out from dawn to dusk.

sentences.zh: 及至巧冶鑄干將之樸，清水焠其鋒，越砥斂其咢，水斷蛟龍，陸剸犀革，忽若彗氾畫塗。

translations.literal: When it reaches a clever smith casting Gan Jiang in the rough, clear water quenches its edge, Yue whetstone gathers its guard; in water it cuts dragons, on land it slices rhinoceros hide—suddenly as a comet sweeping, painting mud.
translations.idiomatic: Hand that smith the rough ingot of a Gan Jiang blade, let him quench the edge in pure water and true the guard on a Yue whetstone, and the finished sword will slice dragons in the river and rhinoceros hide on land as easily as a comet streaks or a brush draws a line in wet plaster.

sentences.zh: 如此，則使離婁督繩，公輸削墨，雖崇臺五增，延袤百丈，而不溷者，工用相得也。

translations.literal: If it is thus, then causing Li Lou to supervise the line and Gongshu to shave the ink—even though lofty terraces increase fivefold, extending a hundred zhang, yet without confusion—it is craftsman and use suiting each other.
translations.idiomatic: Set Li Lou to snap the plumb line and Gongshu Ban to true the ink, and terraces may rise five stories and stretch a hundred yards without the least disorder, for master and tool are perfectly matched.

sentences.zh: 庸人之御駑馬，亦傷吻敝策而不進於行，匈喘膚汗，人極馬倦。

translations.literal: When a mediocre man drives a nag, he also injures the mouth and wears out the whip yet does not advance in the journey; chest pants, skin sweats, man exhausted, horse weary.
translations.idiomatic: Put a plow horse in the hands of a duffer and he will bloody its mouth and splinter his whip without gaining a mile—man panting, horse lathered, both pushed past endurance.

sentences.zh: 及至駕齧錾，驂乘旦，王良執靶，韓哀附輿，縱馳騁騖，忽如景靡，過都越國，蹶如歷塊；

translations.literal: When it reaches harnessing the bit-champ and file, paired horses ride at dawn, Wang Liang grasps the reins, Han Ai attends the carriage, letting loose galloping and racing, suddenly like a shadow melting, passing capitals, crossing states, stumbling as if treading clods;
translations.idiomatic: Yoke mettlesome steeds at dawn, put Wang Liang on the reins and Han Ai beside the box, and the team flies like a dissolving shadow past cities and through whole provinces as though each stride cleared a low hillock;

sentences.zh: 追奔電，逐遺風，周流八極，萬里壹息。

translations.literal: pursue fleeing lightning, chase the vanishing wind, circulate the eight extremities, ten thousand li in one breath.
translations.idiomatic: they race the lightning and hunt the tail of the storm, wheel through the eight directions, and clear ten thousand li between one breath and the next.

sentences.zh: 何其遼哉？

translations.literal: How vast is that!
translations.idiomatic: How boundless that freedom!

sentences.zh: 人馬相得也。

translations.literal: Man and horse suit each other.
translations.idiomatic: Horse and driver have become one.

sentences.zh: 故服絺綌之涼者，不苦盛暑之鬱燠；

translations.literal: Therefore those who wear fine summer silk's coolness do not suffer the sweltering heat of oppressive summer;
translations.idiomatic: He who dons fine summer gauze does not dread the worst dog-day glare;

sentences.zh: 襲貂狐之飕者，不憂至寒之悽愴。

translations.literal: those who don sable and fox's chill wind do not worry about utmost cold's bitter chill.
translations.idiomatic: wrap himself in sable and fox and he forgets the bite of deepest winter.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why is it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 有其具者易其備。

translations.literal: Those who have its implements ease its preparations.
translations.idiomatic: Because the right equipment makes readiness easy.

sentences.zh: 賢人君子，亦聖主之所以易海內也。

translations.literal: Worthy men and gentlemen are also what a sage ruler uses to ease within the seas.
translations.idiomatic: The worthy minister is the sage emperor's tool for bringing ease to the realm.

sentences.zh: 是以嘔喻受之，開寬裕之路，以延天下英俊也。

translations.literal: Therefore he croons and accepts them, opens a road of breadth and surplus, in order to extend to the realm's heroes.
translations.idiomatic: That is why he welcomes them with open encouragement, clears a broad path, and draws the empire's finest minds to his side.

sentences.zh: 夫竭知附賢者，必建仁策；

translations.literal: Those who exhaust wisdom and attach to the worthy surely establish benevolent plans;
translations.idiomatic: A ruler who pours out his wit to serve the worthy will lay plans rooted in humanity;

sentences.zh: 索人求士者，必樹伯跡。

translations.literal: those who search for men and seek knights surely plant hegemon traces.
translations.idiomatic: one who hunts everywhere for talent will leave the footprints of a true king.

sentences.zh: 昔周公躬吐捉之勞，故有圉空之隆；

translations.literal: Formerly the Duke of Zhou personally took the toil of spitting out food and grasping hair; therefore there was the height of empty prisons;
translations.idiomatic: The Duke of Zhou interrupted every meal and shampoo to receive guests, and his reward was an age in which the jails stood empty;

sentences.zh: 齊桓設庭燎之禮，故有匡合之功。

translations.literal: Duke Huan of Qi established the rite of courtyard torches; therefore there was the achievement of rectifying and uniting.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan of Qi lit torches in his courtyard to welcome late-night counsel, and so won the deed of binding the states in alliance.

sentences.zh: 由此觀之，君人者勤於求賢而逸於得人。

translations.literal: From this viewing it, one who rules men is diligent in seeking the worthy and at ease in obtaining men.
translations.idiomatic: From this we see that the sovereign who toils to find ministers may rule at ease once they are found.

sentences.zh: 人臣亦然。

translations.literal: Ministers to others are also thus.
translations.idiomatic: The same holds for those who serve him.

sentences.zh: 昔賢者之未遭遇也，圖事揆策則君不用其謀，陳見悃誠則上不然其信，進仕不得施效，斥逐又非其愆。

translations.literal: Formerly when worthies had not yet met encounter, in plotting affairs and measuring strategies the lord did not use their plans; in displaying and seeing sincere devotion the superior did not deem their trust true; advancing to office they could not display effect; expulsion and banishment again was not their fault.
translations.idiomatic: Before a worthy man meets his moment, the ruler ignores every plan he lays, doubts every pledge of loyalty he offers, denies him office where he might prove himself, and casts him aside for faults that are not his own.

sentences.zh: 是故伊尹勤於鼎俎，太公困於鼓刀，百里自鬻，甯子飯牛，離此患也。

translations.literal: Therefore Yi Yin toiled at cauldrons and stands, Grand Duke Tai was trapped at drumming the knife, Baili sold himself, Ningzi fed oxen—leaving this misfortune.
translations.idiomatic: Yi Yin sweated over the kitchen cauldrons, the Grand Duke Tai peddled from a butcher's block, Baili Xi sold himself into service, Ning Qi sang beside his ox—all to escape that plight.

sentences.zh: 及其遇明君遭聖主也，運籌合上意，諫諍即見聽，進退得關其忠，任職得行其術，去卑辱奧渫而升本朝，離疏釋蹻而享膏粱，剖符錫壤而光祖考，傳之子孫，以資說士。

translations.literal: When they meet a bright lord and encounter a sage master, planning matches the superior's intent, remonstrance is then heard, advance and retreat can connect their loyalty, holding office can enact their methods; they leave base shame and hidden filth and ascend to this court, depart coarse cloth and release straw sandals and enjoy fat and fine millet, split tallies and grant soil and brighten forefathers, transmit to sons and grandsons, thereby supplying persuading knights.
translations.idiomatic: But let them find an enlightened sovereign, and every stratagem strikes home, every remonstrance wins a hearing, every move displays loyalty, every post lets their policy work. They rise from humble toil to stand in the central court, shed hemp shoes for robes of grain-fed silk, take investiture and fief to bring honor to the ancestors, and hand the credit down to their sons as a lesson to every persuader who waits in the cold.

sentences.zh: 故世必有聖知之君，而後有賢明之臣。

translations.literal: Therefore the age must have a sage-and-knowing lord, and afterward there are worthy-and-bright ministers.
translations.idiomatic: So the age must first produce a sage-king; only then can it produce ministers of true clarity.

sentences.zh: 故虎嘯而冽風，龍興而致雲，蟋蟀俟秋吟，蜉蝤出以陰。

translations.literal: Therefore the tiger roars and biting wind comes; the dragon rises and brings clouds; the cricket waits for autumn to chirp; the mayfly emerges with yin.
translations.idiomatic: The tiger's roar summons the biting gale; the dragon's ascent draws the rain clouds; the cricket waits for autumn to sing; the mayfly hatches when the yin ether gathers.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：「飛龍在天，利見大人。」

translations.literal: The Changes say: 'Flying dragon in the sky—it is favorable to see the great man.'
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Changes says, 'The dragon flies in the heavens: it furthers one to see the great man.'

sentences.zh: 《詩》曰：「思皇多士，生此王國。」

translations.literal: The Odes say: 'Oh, august many gentlemen, born in this king's state.'
translations.idiomatic: The Odes sing, 'Splendid host of knights, born for this royal house.'

sentences.zh: 故世平主聖，俊艾將自至，若堯、舜、禹、湯、文、武之君，獲稷、契、皋陶、伊尹、呂望，明明在朝，穆穆列布，聚精會神，相得益章。

translations.literal: Therefore when the age is peaceful and the lord is sage, outstanding elders will arrive of themselves; like the lords Yao, Shun, Yu, Tang, Wen, and Wu, obtaining Ji, Xie, Gao Yao, Yi Yin, and Lü Wang—bright bright in court, solemn solemn arrayed in rows, gathering essence, meeting spirit, mutually obtaining increased splendor.
translations.idiomatic: When the realm is at peace and the throne is wise, great men come unbidden, as when Yao, Shun, Yu, Tang, Wen, and Wu won the service of Hou Ji, Xie, Gao Yao, Yi Yin, and Lü Wang—radiant ministers ranked in solemn order, pooling their insight until ruler and subject shine the brighter together.

sentences.zh: 雖伯牙操遞鍾，逢門子彎烏號，猶未足以喻其意也。

translations.literal: Even if Boya grasped the Di Zhong zither, Fengmenzi bent the Wuhao bow, still they would not be sufficient to analogize its meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Not even Boya on the Di Zhong harp or Fengmenzi drawing the Wuhao bow could capture the harmony of that meeting.

sentences.zh: 故聖主必待賢臣而弘功業，俊士亦俟明主以顯其德。

translations.literal: Therefore the sage lord must await worthy ministers to enlarge merit and enterprise; outstanding knights also await a bright lord to display their virtue.
translations.idiomatic: The sage ruler waits on worthy ministers to magnify his achievement, and the brilliant minister waits on an enlightened throne to give full scope to his virtue.

sentences.zh: 上下俱欲，驩然交欣，千載壹合，論說無疑，翼乎如鴻毛過順風，沛乎如巨魚縱大壑。

translations.literal: Above and below both desire; joyfully they mutually rejoice; a thousand years one meeting; discussion and persuasion without doubt; winging like goose feather passing favorable wind, vast like great fish released in a great ravine.
translations.idiomatic: When sovereign and minister share one will, they rejoice together in a match made once in a millennium, speaking as freely as a feather on a fair wind or a great fish loosed in the ocean trench.

sentences.zh: 其得意若此，則胡禁不止，曷令不行？

translations.literal: If obtaining intent is thus, then what barbarian prohibition does not stop, what command does not go?
translations.idiomatic: At such a height of concord, what could resist your ban or disobey your command?

sentences.zh: 化溢四表，橫被無窮，遐夷貢獻，萬祥畢溱。

translations.literal: Transformation overflows the four outer zones, spreads horizontally without end; distant Yi offer tribute; myriad auspiciousness all gather thick.
translations.idiomatic: Your transforming power spills beyond the four seas, rolls on without limit, draws tribute from the farthest tribes, and heaps every kind of blessing at your feet.

sentences.zh: 是以聖王不遍窺望而視已明，不單頃耳而聽已聰；

translations.literal: Therefore the sage king does not everywhere peep and gaze yet sight is already bright; does not alone incline the ear yet hearing is already keen;
translations.idiomatic: The sage king need not crane his neck in every direction to see clearly, nor strain his ears to hear acutely;

sentences.zh: 恩從祥風翱，德與和氣游，太平之責塞，優游之望得；

translations.literal: grace follows auspicious wind soaring, virtue wanders with harmonious qi, the burden of great peace is filled, the hope of ease is obtained;
translations.idiomatic: his kindness rides the auspicious breeze, his virtue mingles with the harmonious ether, the weight of perfect peace settles on his shoulders, and the dream of ruling at leisure comes true;

sentences.zh: 遵遊自然之勢，恬淡無為之場，休徵自至，壽考無疆，雍容垂拱，永永萬年，何必偃卬詘信若彭祖，呴噓呼吸如僑、松，眇然絕俗離世哉！

translations.literal: Following and roaming the tendency of nature, in the field of quiet plain non-action, auspicious signs arrive of themselves, long life has no border, harmonious ease with folded hands, forever ten thousand years—why must one bend and stretch and twist trust like Pengzu, exhale and inhale like Qiao and Song, subtly cut off custom and leave the world!
translations.idiomatic: he drifts with nature's current in the stillness of wu-wei until good omens come unbidden, his years know no bound, and he may rule with folded hands for endless ages. Why should he need Pengzu's calisthenics or the breathing tricks of Wangzi Qiao and Chisongzi, or cut himself off from the human world to chase immortals?

sentences.zh: 《詩》云「濟濟多士，文王以寧」，蓋信乎其以寧也！

translations.literal: The Odes say, 'Many officers in array—King Wen thereby was tranquil'—indeed trustworthy that thereby he was tranquil!
translations.idiomatic: The Odes say, 'Rank on rank of knights—that was how King Wen kept the realm at peace'—and we may trust every word.

sentences.zh: 是時，上頗好神僊，故褒對及之。

translations.literal: At this time the emperor somewhat favored divine immortals; therefore Bao's reply reached to it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was much taken with immortality cults at the time, so Wang Bao touched the theme in his answer.

sentences.zh: 上令褒與張子僑等並待詔，數從褒等放獵，所幸宮館，輒為歌頌，第其高下，以差賜帛。

translations.literal: The emperor ordered Bao and Zhang Ziqiao and others together to await edict; repeatedly followed Bao and others on release hunts; at favored palaces and lodges, then composed songs and eulogies, ranked their high and low, and by gradation granted silk.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor kept Wang Bao and Zhang Ziqiao on the waiting roster and often took them along on the hunt. Wherever the royal party lodged, Wang Bao would compose a celebratory piece; the emperor ranked the poems and handed out silk according to merit.

sentences.zh: 議者多以為淫靡不急，上曰：「『不有博弈者乎，為之猶賢乎已！』

translations.literal: Discussers mostly thought them dissolute and not urgent; the emperor said: 'Is it not said, "Are there not chess players? Doing that is still better than stopping!"'
translations.idiomatic: Critics dismissed such verse as frivolous and beside the point of government, but the emperor replied, 'Confucius himself asked whether a game of chess was not still better than doing nothing at all.'

sentences.zh: 辭賦大者與古詩同義，小者辯麗可喜。

translations.literal: Ci and fu, large ones share meaning with ancient poems; small ones are eloquent and lovely and pleasing.
translations.idiomatic: The greater rhapsodies pursue the same ends as the ancient odes; the lighter pieces charm with wit and color.

sentences.zh: 辟如女工有綺縠，音樂有鄭衛，今世俗猶皆以此虞說耳目，辭賦比之，尚有仁義風諭，鳥獸草木多聞之觀，賢於倡優博弈遠矣。」

translations.literal: It is like women workers having patterned silk, music having Zheng and Wei; now the vulgar custom still all by this pleases and delights ear and eye; compared to this, ci and fu still have benevolence and rightness wind instruction, birds, beasts, plants, and trees much-heard observation—better than entertainers and chess by far.'
translations.idiomatic: Think of figured damask among weavers or the airs of Zheng and Wei among tunes: the world already delights its eyes and ears with such things. Beside them, rhapsody still carries moral allegory in birds, beasts, plants, and trees—it is infinitely superior to buffoonery or a game of weiqi.'

sentences.zh: 頃之，擢褒為諫大夫。

translations.literal: Before long, Bao was promoted to Grandee Remonstrant.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward Wang Bao was promoted to Grandee Remonstrant.

sentences.zh: 其後太子體不安，苦忽忽善忘，不樂。

translations.literal: Afterward the heir's body was not at ease; he suffered hazy forgetfulness, not joyful.
translations.idiomatic: Later the crown prince fell ill with a restless, forgetful melancholy that would not lift.

sentences.zh: 詔使褒等皆之太子宮虞侍太子，朝夕誦讀奇文及所自造作。

translations.literal: An edict ordered Bao and others all to go to the heir's palace to attend and serve the heir, morning and evening reciting and reading strange texts and what they themselves had composed.
translations.idiomatic: An edict sent Wang Bao and his colleagues to the heir apparent's quarters to divert and attend him, reading aloud day and night from rare literature and from their own compositions.

sentences.zh: 疾平復，乃歸。

translations.literal: When the illness was pacified and restored, then they returned.
translations.idiomatic: When the prince recovered, they were dismissed to their posts.

sentences.zh: 太子喜褒所為甘泉及洞簫頌，令後宮貴人左右皆誦讀之。

translations.literal: The heir was pleased with what Bao had composed, 'Sweet Springs' and 'Eulogy on the Panpipes'; he ordered the rear palace's honored persons left and right all to recite and read them.
translations.idiomatic: The heir especially loved Wang Bao's 'Sweet Springs' rhapsody and his 'Eulogy on the Panpipes' and ordered the palace ladies in attendance to memorize them.

sentences.zh: 後方士言益州有金馬碧雞之寶，可祭祀致也，宣帝使褒往祀焉。

translations.literal: Afterward a fangshi said Yizhou had the treasures of the golden horse and green fowl; they could be brought by sacrifice; Emperor Xuan sent Bao to go sacrifice there.
translations.idiomatic: Later a wizard reported that Yizhou held the spirits of the Golden Horse and Green Cock, which might be summoned by sacrifice. Emperor Xuan dispatched Wang Bao to conduct the rites.

sentences.zh: 褒於道病死，上閔惜之。

translations.literal: Bao died of illness on the road; the emperor pitied and regretted him.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Bao died of illness en route, and the emperor mourned his loss.

sentences.zh: 賈捐之

translations.literal: Jia Juanzhi.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Juanzhi

sentences.zh: 賈捐之字君房，賈誼之曾孫也。

translations.literal: Jia Juanzhi, courtesy name Junfang, was the great-grandson of Jia Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Juanzhi, courtesy name Junfang, was the great-grandson of Jia Yi.

sentences.zh: 元帝初即位，上疏言得失，召待詔金馬門。

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan had first ascended the throne, he submitted a memorial discussing gain and loss; he was summoned to await edict at the Golden Horse Gate.
translations.idiomatic: Soon after Emperor Yuan took the throne, Jia Juanzhi presented a memorial on policy, and the court added him to the scholars awaiting summons at the Golden Horse Gate.

sentences.zh: 初，武帝征南越，元封元年立儋耳、珠崖郡，皆在南方海中洲居，廣袤可千里，合十六縣，戶二萬三千餘。

translations.literal: At first, Emperor Wu campaigned against Southern Yue; in the first year of Yuanfeng he established Dan'er and Zhuya commanderies; both dwelt on islets in the sea to the south, breadth and length perhaps a thousand li, together sixteen counties, households a little over twenty-three thousand.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Wu conquered Southern Yue, he founded the commanderies of Dan'er and Zhuya in the first year of Yuanfeng. They lay on islands in the southern sea, stretched perhaps a thousand li from end to end, comprised sixteen counties, and counted a little over twenty-three thousand households.

sentences.zh: 其民暴惡，自以阻絕，數犯吏禁，吏亦酷之，率數年壹反，殺吏，漢輒發兵擊定之。

translations.literal: Their people were violent and wicked; relying on barrier isolation, they repeatedly violated clerical prohibitions; the clerks also were cruel to them; on average every several years once they rebelled, killed clerks, and Han always raised troops to strike and pacify them.
translations.idiomatic: The inhabitants were fierce and lawless, confident that distance shielded them. They broke official regulations again and again, and the magistrates repaid them with cruelty. Every few years they would rise, murder Han officials, and prompt a punitive expedition from the mainland.

sentences.zh: 自初為郡至昭帝始元元年，二十餘年間，凡六反叛。

translations.literal: From first becoming commanderies to Emperor Zhao's first year of Shiyuan, in more than twenty years, there were six rebellions in all.
translations.idiomatic: Between the founding of the commanderies and Emperor Zhao's first Shiyuan year—barely twenty years—there were six full-scale revolts.

sentences.zh: 至其五年，罷儋耳郡并屬珠崖。

translations.literal: By its fifth year, Dan'er commandery was abolished and merged to belong to Zhuya.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifth year of that reign Dan'er was abolished and its territory folded into Zhuya.

sentences.zh: 至宣帝神爵三年，珠崖三縣復反。

translations.literal: By Emperor Xuan's third year of Shenjue, three counties of Zhuya again rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan, in the third Shenjue year, three Zhuya counties rose again.

sentences.zh: 反後七年，甘露元年，九縣反，輒發兵擊定之。

translations.literal: Seven years after the rebellion, in the first year of Ganlu, nine counties rebelled; troops were immediately raised to strike and pacify them.
translations.idiomatic: Seven years later, in the first Ganlu year, nine counties mutinied and were again beaten down by Han arms.

sentences.zh: 元帝初元元年，珠崖又反，發兵擊之。

translations.literal: Emperor Yuan's first year of Chuyuan, Zhuya again rebelled; troops were raised to strike them.
translations.idiomatic: In Emperor Yuan's first Chuyuan year Zhuya rebelled once more, and the court sent troops against it.

sentences.zh: 諸縣更叛，連年不定。

translations.literal: The various counties rebelled in turn; year after year it was not settled.
translations.idiomatic: County after county changed sides; the turmoil dragged on without end.

sentences.zh: 上與有司議大發軍，捐之建議，以為不當擊。

translations.literal: The emperor with the responsible officials discussed greatly raising troops; Juanzhi proposed a plan, thinking one ought not strike.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor and his ministers debated a major mobilization, but Jia Juanzhi argued that conquest was the wrong course.

sentences.zh: 上使侍中駙馬都尉樂昌侯王商詰問捐之曰：「珠崖內屬為郡久矣，今背畔逆節，而云不當擊，長蠻夷之亂，虧先帝功德，經義何以處之？」

translations.literal: The emperor sent Palace Attendant, Commandant of Household Cavalry, Marquis of Yuechang Wang Shang to examine and question Juanzhi, saying: 'Zhuya has long been internally submissive as a commandery; now it turns its back and violates norm; yet you say one ought not strike—lengthening the barbarian Yi's disorder, diminishing the former emperor's merit and virtue—how does the meaning of the classics dispose of this?'
