translations.idiomatic: The emperor ordered Wang Shang, the marquis of Yuechang, who served as palace attendant and commandant of household cavalry, to confront Jia Juanzhi. 'Zhuya has been an internal commandery for ages,' he said. 'Now it breaks faith. To refuse a campaign is to encourage barbarian revolt and blot the achievement of the late emperor. What do the classics say to that?'

sentences.zh: 捐之對曰：

translations.literal: Juanzhi replied, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Jia Juanzhi replied:

sentences.zh: 臣幸得遭明盛之朝，蒙危言之策，無忌諱之患，敢昧死竭卷卷。

translations.literal: Your servant has been fortunate enough to encounter a bright and flourishing court, to receive a policy of perilous words, without the misfortune of taboo; dares in darkness of death to exhaust earnestness.
translations.idiomatic: I am blessed to serve an enlightened age that welcomes blunt counsel and imposes no muzzle on loyal speech. I will risk my life to speak plainly.

sentences.zh: 臣聞堯舜，聖之盛也，禹入聖域而不優，故孔子稱堯曰「大哉」，韶曰「盡善」，禹曰「無間」。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that Yao and Shun were the height of sagehood; Yu entered the sage domain yet was not superior; therefore Confucius called Yao 'great indeed,' the Shao 'utterly good,' Yu 'without flaw.'
translations.idiomatic: Yao and Shun stood at the summit of sagehood; Yu entered that company yet did not surpass them—which is why Confucius called Yao 'magnificent,' the Shao music 'perfect in goodness,' and Yu 'without blemish.'

sentences.zh: 以三聖之德，地方不過數千里，被流沙，東漸于海，朔南暨聲教，迄于四海，欲與聲教則治之，不欲與者不彊治也。

translations.literal: By the virtue of the three sages, their territory was not more than several thousand li square; it covered the shifting sands, east reached to the sea, north and south arrived at sound and instruction, reaching to the four seas; those who wished to share sound and instruction they governed; those who did not wish to share, they did not force to govern.
translations.idiomatic: Even those three sages held only a few thousand li of ground—west to the drifting sands, east to the sea—yet their moral voice reached north and south to the rim of the world. They ruled only those who welcomed their teaching and never forced it on those who did not.

sentences.zh: 故君臣歌德，含氣之物各德其宜。

translations.literal: Therefore lord and ministers sang virtue; things bearing qi each obtained their proper virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Ruler and ministers could hymn one another's virtue, and every living creature found its proper place.

sentences.zh: 武丁、成王，殷、周之大仁也，然地東不過江、黃，西不過氐、羌，南不過蠻荊，北不過朔方。

translations.literal: Wu Ding and King Cheng were the great benevolence of Yin and Zhou; yet their lands east did not pass Jiang and Huang, west did not pass Di and Qiang, south did not pass Man Jing, north did not pass Shuofang.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Ding of Shang and King Cheng of Zhou were paragons of humanity, yet Shang territory reached east only to the Jiang and Huang domains, west only to the Di and Qiang, south only to the Man tribes of Jing, and north only to Shuofang.

sentences.zh: 是以頌聲並作，視聽之類咸樂其生，越裳氏重九譯而獻，此非兵革之所能致。

translations.literal: Therefore eulogy voices arose together; kinds of seeing and hearing all rejoiced in their lives; the Yueshang clan passed nine interpreters and offered tribute—this is not what arms and leather could bring about.
translations.idiomatic: Praise filled the air, every creature rejoiced in its life, and the Yueshang people sent tribute through nine relays of interpreters—gifts won by virtue, not by the sword.

sentences.zh: 及其衰也，南征不還，齊桓捄其難，孔子定其文。

translations.literal: When it came to their decline, the southern expedition did not return; Duke Huan of Qi rescued its difficulty; Confucius fixed its text.
translations.idiomatic: In decline, kings marched south and never came home; Duke Huan of Qi had to rescue the house of Zhou, and Confucius set the record straight in the Spring and Autumn Annals.

sentences.zh: 以至乎秦，興兵遠攻，貪外虛內，務欲廣地，不慮其害。

translations.literal: Reaching to Qin, it raised troops and attacked far, greedy outside and empty inside, striving to widen territory, not considering its harm.
translations.idiomatic: Then came Qin, which launched distant wars, starved the interior to feed ambition, and grabbed land without counting the cost.

sentences.zh: 然地南不過閩越，北不過太原，而天下潰畔，禍卒在於二世之末，長城之歌至今未絕。

translations.literal: Yet their land south did not pass Min-Yue, north did not pass Taiyuan, yet the realm collapsed and rebelled; disaster finally at the end of the Second Generation; the song of the Great Wall to this day has not ceased.
translations.idiomatic: Even so Qin never pushed south beyond Min-Yue or north beyond Taiyuan, yet the empire shattered. Calamity struck under the Second Emperor, and the ballad of the Great Wall still haunts us.

sentences.zh: 賴聖漢初興，為百姓請命，平定天下。

translations.literal: Relying on sage Han's first rise, it pleaded life for the hundred surnames and pacified the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Our sacred Han rose in mercy, pleaded Heaven for the people's lives, and brought peace to the world.

sentences.zh: 至孝文皇帝，閔中國未安，偃武行文，則斷獄數百，民賦四十，丁男三年而一事。

translations.literal: Reaching Emperor Xiaowen, he pitied that the central states were not yet secure; laid down arms and raised culture; then cases judged were in the hundreds, people's tax forty, adult males once in three years one affair.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen, grieving that the heartland was still unsettled, sheathed the sword and promoted civil rule until court dockets numbered only in the hundreds, the poll tax fell to forty cash, and each able-bodied man owed the state only one month's labor every three years.

sentences.zh: 時有獻千里馬者，詔曰：「鸞旗在前，屬車在後，吉行日五十里，師行二十里，朕乘千里之馬，獨先安之？」

translations.literal: At that time there was one who presented a thousand-li horse; an edict said: 'The phoenix banner is in front, the attendant carriages behind; auspicious travel is fifty li a day, army travel twenty li—if I ride a thousand-li horse, alone where would I go first?'
translations.idiomatic: When someone offered a horse said to cover a thousand li a day, the emperor replied with an edict: 'The imperial pennons lead and the train follows; on a peaceful journey I cover fifty li a day, on campaign twenty. Where under Heaven could I gallop alone on such a beast?'

sentences.zh: 於是還馬，與道里費，而下詔曰：「朕不受獻也，其令四方毋求來獻。」

translations.literal: Thereupon he returned the horse, gave travel expenses, and issued an edict saying: 'We do not accept offerings; order the four directions not to seek to come offering.'
translations.idiomatic: He sent the horse back with money for the donor's journey and proclaimed, 'I accept no gifts. Let no region send tribute of this kind again.'

sentences.zh: 當此之時，逸游之樂絕，奇麗之賂塞，鄭衛之倡微矣。

translations.literal: At this time, wandering pleasure was cut off, strange and lovely bribes were blocked, Zheng and Wei entertainers became slight.
translations.idiomatic: At that moment wasteful pastimes died away, lavish bribes dried up, and the lewd music of Zheng and Wei faded from court.

sentences.zh: 夫後官盛色則賢者隱處，佞人用事則諍臣杜口，而文帝不行，故諡為孝文，廟稱太宗。

translations.literal: When the rear palace abounds in beauty, then worthies hide; when fawning men handle affairs, then remonstrating ministers shut their mouths; yet Emperor Wen did not practice these; therefore his posthumous name was Xiaowen, his temple was called Taizong.
translations.idiomatic: A harem crowded with beauties drives the good into hiding; when sycophants hold power, honest advisers fall silent. Emperor Wen would have none of it, which is why posterity calls him 'the Filial and Cultured' and honors his shrine as Taizong.

sentences.zh: 至孝武皇帝元狩六年，太倉之粟紅腐而不可食，都內之錢貫朽而不可挍。

translations.literal: Reaching Emperor Xiaowu's sixth year of Yuanshou, the Grand Granary's grain was red-rotten and could not be eaten; the capital's inner cash strings were rotten and could not be counted.
translations.idiomatic: By the sixth Yuanshou year of Emperor Wu the imperial granaries held grain that had rotted to pink dust, and the strings of cash in the capital vaults had decayed so that the coins could not be tallied.

sentences.zh: 乃探平城之事，錄冒頓以來數為邊害，籍兵厲馬，因富民以攘服之。

translations.literal: Then he investigated the affair of Pingcheng, recorded since Modu repeatedly being border harm, registered troops and sharpened horses, relying on rich people in order to seize and subdue them.
translations.idiomatic: He brooded on the humiliation of Pingcheng, reviewed how Modu and his heirs had ravaged the frontier, mobilized armies and bred horses, and drew on the wealthy to fund conquest until the steppe submitted.

sentences.zh: 西連諸國至于安息，東過碣石以玄菟、樂浪為郡，比卻匈奴萬里，更起營塞，制南海以為八郡，則天下斷獄萬數，民賦數百，造鹽鐵酒榷之利以佐用度，猶不能足。

translations.literal: West he linked the various states reaching to Anxi; east he passed Jieshi and made Xuantu and Lelang commanderies; pressing back the Xiongnu ten thousand li, repeatedly raising camps and barriers, controlling the South Sea to make eight commanderies—then cases judged in the realm were in the ten thousands, people's taxes in the hundreds; he created salt, iron, and wine monopoly profits to assist expenditures, yet still could not suffice.
translations.idiomatic: He linked the western states as far as Parthia, pushed east beyond Jieshi to found Xuantu and Lelang, drove the Xiongnu back ten thousand li, threw up a chain of fortresses, and carved eight commanderies from the southern sea. Court cases mounted into the tens of thousands, the poll tax into the hundreds of cash, and even the monopolies on salt, iron, and wine could not fill the treasury.

sentences.zh: 當此之時，寇賊並起，軍旅數發，父戰死於前，子鬥傷於後，女子乘亭鄣，孤兒號於道，老母寡婦飲泣巷哭，遙設虛祭，想魂乎萬里之外。

translations.literal: At this time, bandits and thieves arose together; armies repeatedly set out; fathers died in battle in front, sons fought wounded behind; women mounted watchtowers; orphans wailed on the roads; old mothers and widows swallowed tears and wept in lanes; from afar they set empty sacrifices, imagining souls ten thousand li beyond.
translations.idiomatic: Banditry flared everywhere. Campaign followed campaign: fathers fell in the van while sons bled in the rear, women mounted the signal towers of frontier posts, orphans screamed along the highways, and gray-haired widows wept in the alleys, offering ghost feasts to souls lost ten thousand li away.

sentences.zh: 淮南王盜寫虎符，陰聘名士，關東公孫勇等詐為使者，是皆廓地泰大，征伐不休之故也。

translations.literal: The king of Huainan secretly copied the tiger tally, secretly hired famous knights; east of the passes Gongsun Yong and others falsely acted as envoys—these all were because territory was expanded too greatly and campaigns did not cease.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Huainan forged the tiger tally and hired secret partisans; east of the passes men such as Gongsun Yong posed as imperial envoys. All of it sprang from overgrown frontiers and endless war.

sentences.zh: 今天下獨有關東，關東大者獨有齊楚，民眾久困，連年流離，離其城郭，相枕席於道路。

translations.literal: Now the realm has only east of the passes; east of the passes the great ones are only Qi and Chu; the masses have long been distressed, year after year drifting and parting, leaving their walls and moats, pillowing mats on one another on the roads.
translations.idiomatic: Today all that truly sustains the dynasty lies east of the passes, and the chief weight there falls on Qi and Chu. The people have suffered too long: year after year they flee their homes and sleep in heaps along the roads.

sentences.zh: 人情莫親父母，莫樂夫婦，至嫁妻賣子，法不能禁，義不能止，此社稷之憂也。

translations.literal: In human feeling none is closer than father and mother, none happier than husband and wife; reaching to selling wives and selling children, law cannot prohibit, rightness cannot stop—this is the altars' worry.
translations.idiomatic: Nothing binds the heart like parent and child or husband and wife—yet we now see wives sold and children pawned, beyond the reach of law or shame. This is a crisis for the altars themselves.

sentences.zh: 今陛下不忍悁悁之忿，欲驅士眾擠之大海之中，快心幽冥之地，非所以救助飢饉，保全元元也。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty cannot bear fretting anger, wishes to drive soldiers and masses to press them into the midst of the great sea, gratify the heart in dark hidden places—it is not the means to rescue famine and hunger, preserve and complete the black-haired people.
translations.idiomatic: To indulge a private grudge and march an army into the wastes of the southern sea, chasing satisfaction in a barbarous gloom, is no way to relieve famine or save the common people.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云「蠢爾蠻荊，大邦為讎」，言聖人起則後服，中國衰則先畔，動為國家難，自古而患之久矣，何況乃復其南方萬里之蠻乎！

translations.literal: The Odes say, 'Stupid you, Man Jing, the great state is your foe'—meaning when sages arise then afterward they submit, when the central states decline then first they rebel; moving, they become difficulty for the state; from antiquity this has long been a worry—how much more then again the southern ten-thousand-li Man!
translations.idiomatic: The Odes call the Jing tribes 'crafty southern savages, enemies of the great state'—they submit only when a sage-king holds the throne and are first to rebel when China weakens. They have been a running sore since high antiquity; how much more those savages ten thousand li to the south!

sentences.zh: 駱越之人父子同川而浴，相習以鼻飲，與禽獸無異，本不足郡縣置也。

translations.literal: The Luo-Yue people, fathers and sons bathe in the same stream together, are habituated to drinking through the nose; they are no different from birds and beasts; originally they were not worth establishing commanderies and counties.
translations.idiomatic: The Luo-Yue bathe in the same streams with their sons and sip liquor through their noses like animals. They were never fit subjects for Chinese magistrates.

sentences.zh: 顓顓獨居一海之中，霧露氣溼，多毒草蟲蛇水土之害，人未見虜，戰士自死。

translations.literal: Alone they dwell in the midst of one sea; fog and dew, qi damp; many poisonous plants, insects, snakes, water, and soil harms; people have not yet seen captives, warriors die of themselves.
translations.idiomatic: They squat alone beyond the sea in fog and rot, among venomous plants, snakes, and tainted water. Our soldiers perish of disease before they ever sight an enemy.

sentences.zh: 又非獨珠崖有珠犀玳瑁也，棄之不足惜，不擊不損威。

translations.literal: Moreover it is not only Zhuya that has pearls, rhinoceros, and tortoiseshell; discarding it is not worth regretting; not striking does not harm awe.
translations.idiomatic: Nor does Zhuya alone yield pearls, rhino horn, and hawksbill. To give it up costs little; to leave it unsubdued costs the throne no true prestige.

sentences.zh: 其民譬猶魚鱉，何足貪也！

translations.literal: Their people are comparable to fish and turtles—what is sufficient to be greedy for!
translations.idiomatic: They are fish and turtles in the sea—hardly worth a greedy war!

sentences.zh: 臣竊以往者羌軍言之，暴師曾未一年，兵出不踰千里，費四十餘萬萬，大司農錢盡，乃以少府禁錢續之。

translations.literal: Your servant privately takes the former Qiang campaign to speak of it: exposed troops not yet one year, troops sent out not exceeding a thousand li, cost more than forty yi, the Grand Minister of Agriculture's cash was exhausted, then used the Privy Treasurer's forbidden cash to continue it.
translations.idiomatic: Consider the recent Qiang war: the army was in the field less than a year and never marched a thousand li, yet it swallowed more than four billion cash, emptied the agriculture ministry's vaults, and forced the palace treasury to make up the deficit.

sentences.zh: 夫一隅為不善，費尚如此，況於勞師遠攻，亡士毋功乎！

translations.literal: When one corner does not good, expense is still thus—how much more laboring the army in distant attack, losing soldiers without merit!
translations.idiomatic: If a single corner of the realm could burn so much treasure, what would a distant expedition cost in blood and bronze for no gain at all?

sentences.zh: 求之往古則不合，施之當今又不便。

translations.literal: Seeking it in past antiquity then it does not match; applying it to the present is also not convenient.
translations.idiomatic: The policy fits neither ancient precedent nor present need.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為非冠帶之國，禹貢所及，春秋所治，皆可且無以為。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks that what are not cap-and-girdle states, what the Tribute of Yu reaches, what the Spring and Autumn governs—all may for the moment be left undone.
translations.idiomatic: I would leave unmolested every land that wears neither cap nor sash, lies beyond the world charted in the Tribute of Yu, and falls outside the moral geography of the Spring and Autumn Annals.

sentences.zh: 願遂棄珠崖，專用恤關東為憂。

translations.literal: I wish thereupon to abandon Zhuya, and devote application to pitying east of the passes as worry.
translations.idiomatic: Abandon Zhuya outright and bend every concern to the starving east of the passes.

sentences.zh: 對奏，上以問丞相御史。

translations.literal: When the reply was presented, the emperor thereupon asked the chancellor and Imperial Clerk.
translations.idiomatic: When the memorial was read, the emperor referred it to the chancellor and the Imperial Counselor.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫陳萬年以為當擊；

translations.literal: Imperial Counselor Chen Wannian thought one ought to strike;
translations.idiomatic: Imperial Counselor Chen Wannian urged a full campaign;

sentences.zh: 丞相于定國以為「前日興兵擊之連年，護軍都尉、校尉及丞凡十一人，還者二人，卒士及轉輸死者萬人以上，費用三萬萬餘，尚未能盡降。

translations.literal: Chancellor Yu Dingguo thought: 'The other day raising troops to strike them year after year—Protecting Army commandant, colonels, and assistants altogether eleven men, those who returned were two; soldiers and transport dead exceeded ten thousand persons; expenses were more than three yi; still could not completely subdue.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Yu Dingguo replied: 'We have already campaigned year after year. Of eleven generals, colonels, and deputies sent, only two came back alive. More than ten thousand soldiers and transport laborers died, and the cost passed three billion—yet the islanders are not fully subdued.

sentences.zh: 今關東困乏，民難搖動，捐之議是。」

translations.literal: Now east of the passes is exhausted and depleted; the people are hard to shake and move—Juanzhi's proposal is right.
translations.idiomatic: The east is exhausted; the people cannot bear another shock. Jia Juanzhi is right.

sentences.zh: 上乃從之。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon followed it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted their advice.

sentences.zh: 遂下詔曰：「珠崖虜殺吏民，背畔為逆，今廷議者或言可擊，或言可守，或欲棄之，其指各殊。

translations.literal: Thereupon an edict was issued, saying: 'Zhuya captives killed clerks and people, turned their backs and rebelled as traitors; now those who court discuss say some may strike, some may guard, some wish to abandon them—their aims each differ.'
translations.idiomatic: He then promulgated an edict: 'The people of Zhuya have murdered our officials and risen in treason. At court some counsel attack, some garrison, some abandonment—three irreconcilable views.'

sentences.zh: 朕日夜惟思議者之言，羞威不行，則欲誅之；

translations.literal: We day and night ponder the discussers' words; ashamed that awe does not go forth, then wish to punish them;
translations.idiomatic: Day and night I weigh those arguments. Shame at impotent prestige tempts me to extirpate them by force;

sentences.zh: 狐疑辟難，則守屯田；

translations.literal: hesitating and avoiding difficulty, then guard garrison fields;
translations.idiomatic: doubt and fear of the cost tempt me to dig in with garrison farms;

sentences.zh: 通于時變，則憂萬民。

translations.literal: penetrating to timely change, then worry for the myriad people.
translations.idiomatic: a sense of what the times allow turns my heart to the millions who suffer hunger.

sentences.zh: 夫萬民之饑餓，與遠蠻之不討，危孰大焉？

translations.literal: As for the myriad people's hunger and famine, versus distant barbarians not punished—which danger is greater?
translations.idiomatic: Which is the greater peril—the hunger of my people or an unpunished tribe beyond the sea?

sentences.zh: 且宗廟之祭，凶年不備，況乎辟不嫌之辱哉！

translations.literal: Moreover as for the ancestral temple's sacrifices, in bad years they are not complete—how much more then avoiding a shame that is not suspect!
translations.idiomatic: Even ancestral offerings are curtailed in years of famine; how can I court the shame of chasing a pointless slight to the ends of the earth?

sentences.zh: 今關東大困，倉庫空虛，無以相贍，又以動兵，非特勞民，凶年隨之。

translations.literal: Now east of the passes is greatly distressed, granaries and treasuries are empty and void, there is nothing by which to aid one another; again to move troops—not only laboring the people, bad years follow it.
translations.idiomatic: The east is in desperate straits; granaries are bare and there is nothing left to feed the people. To mobilize another army would not merely exhaust them—it would invite famine in its train.

sentences.zh: 其罷珠崖郡。

translations.literal: Let Zhuya commandery be abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Therefore I abolish the commandery of Zhuya.

sentences.zh: 民有慕義欲內屬，便處之；

translations.literal: Among the people, those who admire rightness and wish internal submission, conveniently settle them;
translations.idiomatic: Those islanders who wish in good faith to submit may be settled where convenient;

sentences.zh: 不欲，勿彊。」

translations.literal: those who do not wish, do not force.
translations.idiomatic: those who do not wish it shall not be compelled.

sentences.zh: 珠崖由是罷。

translations.literal: Zhuya was thereupon abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Thus Zhuya was abandoned.

sentences.zh: 捐之數召見，言多納用。

translations.literal: Juanzhi was repeatedly summoned and seen; his words were mostly adopted and used.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Juanzhi was summoned often, and much of his advice was taken.

sentences.zh: 時中書令石顯用事，捐之數短顯，以故不得官，後稀復見。

translations.literal: At that time Palace Secretary Shi Xian handled affairs; Juanzhi repeatedly exposed Xian's faults; for this reason he did not obtain office; afterward he was rarely again seen.
translations.idiomatic: Palace Secretary Shi Xian held power at the time, and Jia Juanzhi repeatedly attacked him in memorials—so he never won a substantive post and was seldom called to audience again.

sentences.zh: 而長安令楊興新以材能得幸，與捐之相善。

translations.literal: But the magistrate of Chang'an, Yang Xing, had newly by talent and ability obtained favor, and was on good terms with Juanzhi.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xing, the magistrate of Chang'an, had lately won favor through his abilities and was on close terms with Jia Juanzhi.

sentences.zh: 捐之欲得召見，謂興曰：「京兆尹缺，使我得見，言君蘭，京兆尹可立得。」

translations.literal: Juanzhi wished to obtain a summons and audience; he said to Xing: 'The Metropolitan Governor is vacant; if you let me obtain an audience and speak of Junlan, the Metropolitan Governorship can be immediately obtained.'
translations.idiomatic: Jia Juanzhi longed for another summons. He told Yang Xing, 'The governorship of the capital is open. Give me a chance to praise you to the throne, Junlan, and you can have that office at once.'

sentences.zh: 興曰：「縣官嘗言興瘉薛大夫，我易助也。

translations.literal: Xing said: 'The county official once said Xing is superior to Minister Xue—I am easy to help.'
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xing replied, 'The emperor has said I outrank Minister Xue—so you will find me an easy ally.'

sentences.zh: 君房下筆，言語妙天下，使君房為尚書令，勝五鹿充宗遠甚。」

translations.literal: Junfang's brush—speech and words subtle under heaven; if Junfang were made Palace Secretary, he would surpass Wulu Chongzong by far.
translations.idiomatic: Your pen and tongue are the finest in the realm. Were you made palace secretary, you would put Wulu Chongzong to shame.

sentences.zh: 捐之曰：「令我得代充宗，君蘭為京兆，京兆郡國首，尚書百官本，天下真大治，士則不隔矣。

translations.literal: Juanzhi said: 'If I am able to replace Chongzong, and Junlan becomes Metropolitan Governor—the Metropolitan Governor is the head of commanderies and kingdoms, the Palace Secretary is the root of the hundred officials—then the realm would truly be greatly ordered, and knights would not be blocked.'
translations.idiomatic: Jia Juanzhi said, 'If I replace Chongzong and you, Junlan, take the capital governorship—the premier local post—and I hold the secretariat—the spine of the bureaucracy—the empire would be truly well ruled and no talent would languish in obscurity.'

sentences.zh: 捐之前言平恩侯可為將軍，期思侯並可為諸曹，皆如言；

translations.literal: Juanzhi formerly said the Marquis of Ping'en could be general, the Marquis of Qisi together could be various bureaus—all as he said;
translations.idiomatic: I once said the marquis of Ping'en should be made a general and the marquis of Qisi given bureau posts—and it happened just as I predicted;

sentences.zh: 又薦謁者滿宣，立為冀州刺史；

translations.literal: again recommended Herald Man Xuan, who was immediately made Governor of Ji;
translations.idiomatic: I recommended the herald Man Xuan, who was promptly named governor of Ji;

sentences.zh: 言中謁者不宜受事，宦者不宜入宗廟，立止。

translations.literal: said palace heralds ought not receive affairs, eunuchs ought not enter the ancestral temple—it was immediately stopped.
translations.idiomatic: I argued that inner heralds should not handle state business and that eunuchs should not enter the ancestral shrine—and the court stopped the practice at once.

sentences.zh: 相薦之信，不當如是乎！」

translations.literal: The trust of mutual recommendation—should it not be thus!
translations.idiomatic: Surely men who recommend each other ought to keep faith like that!

sentences.zh: 興曰：「我復見，言君房也。」

translations.literal: Xing said: 'When I am again seen, I will speak of Junfang.'
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xing said, 'On my next audience I will speak up for you.'

sentences.zh: 捐之復短石顯。

translations.literal: Juanzhi again exposed Shi Xian's faults.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Juanzhi again launched a tirade against Shi Xian.

sentences.zh: 興曰：「顯鼎貴，上信用之。

translations.literal: Xing said: 'Xian is tripod-noble; the superior trusts and employs him.'
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xing said, 'Shi Xian stands at the summit of power; the emperor trusts him utterly.'

sentences.zh: 今欲進，弟從我計，且與合意，即得入矣。」

translations.literal: Now if you wish to advance, younger brother follow my plan; for the moment join his intent, then you will be able to enter.
translations.idiomatic: If you want preferment, brother, follow my advice: humor him for now, and you will win your way in.

sentences.zh: 捐之即與興共為薦顯奏，曰：「竊見石顯本山東名族，有禮義之家也。

translations.literal: Juanzhi thereupon with Xing together composed a memorial recommending Xian, saying: 'We have seen that Shi Xian originally is a famous clan of east of the mountains, a household with ritual and rightness.'
translations.idiomatic: Jia Juanzhi then drafted with Yang Xing a joint memorial praising Shi Xian: 'We observe that Shi Xian springs from a great Shandong lineage, a house schooled in ritual and duty.'

sentences.zh: 持正六年，未嘗有過，明習於事，敏而疾見，出公門，入私門。

translations.literal: Upholding rectitude six years, never having fault, clearly practiced in affairs, keen and quick to appear, out the public gate, in the private gate.
translations.idiomatic: For six years he has upheld integrity without a stain, knows every detail of government, is quick and perceptive, and moves straight from the yamen to his private quarters without detour.

sentences.zh: 宜賜爵關內侯，引其兄弟以為諸曹。」

translations.literal: It is fitting to grant him rank as marquis within the passes, draw his brothers to be various bureaus.
translations.idiomatic: He should be ennobled as a marquis within the passes, and his brothers appointed to the chief bureaus.

sentences.zh: 又共為薦興奏，曰：「竊見長安令興，幸得以知名數召見。

translations.literal: Again together they composed a memorial recommending Xing, saying: 'We have seen the magistrate of Chang'an, Xing, fortunate enough to become known and repeatedly summoned and seen.'
translations.idiomatic: They also filed a joint memorial for Yang Xing: 'We note that the magistrate of Chang'an has won fame and repeated audiences with Your Majesty.'

sentences.zh: 興事父母有曾氏之孝，事師有顏閔之材，榮名聞於四方。

translations.literal: Xing serves father and mother with the Zeng clan's filiality, serves teachers with Yan and Min's talent; glorious name is heard in the four directions.
translations.idiomatic: He honors his parents with the devotion of Zeng Shen, serves his teachers with the gifts of Yan Hui and Min Sun, and his good name rings through the realm.

sentences.zh: 明詔舉茂材，列侯以為首。

translations.literal: On the bright edict summoning outstanding talent, ranked marquises placed him first.
translations.idiomatic: When Your Majesty called for men of outstanding talent, the ranked nobles put him at the head of the list.

sentences.zh: 為長安令，吏民敬鄉，道路皆稱能。

translations.literal: As magistrate of Chang'an, clerks and people respectfully turn toward him; on the roads all praise his ability.
translations.idiomatic: As magistrate of Chang'an he commands the reverence of officials and commoners alike, and travelers on every road praise his competence.

sentences.zh: 觀其下筆屬文，則董仲舒；

translations.literal: Observe his setting brush and composing text—then Dong Zhongshu;
translations.idiomatic: Judge him by his prose and you find another Dong Zhongshu;

sentences.zh: 進談動辭，則東方生；

translations.literal: advance talk and moving phrases—then Master Dongfang;
translations.idiomatic: listen to his wit in conversation and you hear Dongfang Shuo;

sentences.zh: 置之爭臣，則汲直；

translations.literal: place him among contending ministers—then Ji Zhi;
translations.idiomatic: set him among blunt remonstrators and he matches Ji An;

sentences.zh: 用之介冑，則冠軍侯；

translations.literal: employ him in armor and helmet—then the Champion Marquis;
translations.idiomatic: put him in armor and he rivals the Champion Marquis;

sentences.zh: 施之治民，則趙廣漢；

translations.literal: apply him to governing the people—then Zhao Guanghan;
translations.idiomatic: give him a district and he governs like Zhao Guanghan;

sentences.zh: 抱公絕私，則尹翁歸。

translations.literal: embracing public and cutting off private—then Yin Wenggui.
translations.idiomatic: charge him to serve the public and spurn private interest and he is another Yin Wenggui.

sentences.zh: 興兼此六人而有之，守道堅固，執義不回，臨大節而不可奪，國之良臣也，可試守京兆尹。」

translations.literal: Xing combines these six men and possesses them; keeping the Way firm and solid, grasping rightness without turning; facing great norms and not being wrested away—he is the state's good minister; may he be tried as acting Metropolitan Governor.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xing unites the virtues of all six: he holds fast to principle, bends duty for no man, and cannot be swayed at the moment of truth. He is a pillar of the state and should be appointed acting metropolitan governor.

sentences.zh: 石顯聞知，白之上。

translations.literal: Shi Xian heard and knew, reported to the superior.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Xian learned of the scheme and denounced them to the emperor.

sentences.zh: 乃下興、捐之獄，令皇后父陽平侯禁與顯共雜治，奏「興、捐之懷詐偽，以上語相風，更相薦譽，欲得大位，漏泄省中語，岡上不道。

translations.literal: Thereupon Xing and Juanzhi were sent down to prison; ordered the empress's father, Marquis of Yangping Wang Jin, together with Xian jointly to try mixedly; memorialized: 'Xing and Juanzhi harbor deceit and falsehood, using superior words to signal each other, alternately recommending and praising, wishing to obtain great position, leaking and divulging inner-palace words, deceiving the superior, conduct that is heinous and disloyal.
translations.idiomatic: Both men were jailed. The emperor assigned the empress's father, Wang Jin, marquis of Yangping, and Shi Xian to hear the case jointly. They reported that Yang Xing and Jia Juanzhi had plotted in fraud, trading confidential phrases from the throne to cue each other, exchanging glowing recommendations in hope of high office, leaking palace secrets, and deceiving their sovereign—conduct that violated every principle of loyalty.

sentences.zh: 書曰：『讒說殄行，震驚朕師。』

translations.literal: The document says: "Slanderous talk and extinguished conduct alarm Our hosts."
translations.idiomatic: The Documents say, "Slanderous words and vile deeds shock my hosts of officers."

sentences.zh: 王制：『順非而澤，不聽而誅。』

translations.literal: Royal Regulations: "Following wrong yet enriching it—do not listen but punish."
translations.idiomatic: The Royal Regulations add, "Whoever glosses evil with fair speech shall be executed without a hearing."

sentences.zh: 請論如法。」

translations.literal: We request judgment according to law.
translations.idiomatic: We ask sentence under the statute.

sentences.zh: 捐之竟坐棄市。

translations.literal: Juanzhi in the end sat for abandonment in the market.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Juanzhi was executed in the marketplace.

sentences.zh: 興減死罪一等，髡鉗為城旦。

translations.literal: Xing had death crime reduced one grade, shaved and collared as wall dawn laborer.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xing's sentence was commuted one degree from death to shaved head, iron collar, and hard labor on the walls.

sentences.zh: 成帝時，至部刺史。

translations.literal: In Emperor Cheng's time, he reached regional inspector.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Cheng he rose to become a regional inspector.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：《詩》稱「戎狄是膺，荊舒是懲」，久矣其為諸夏患也。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: The Odes declare 'the Rong and Di are to be struck, Jing and Shu are to be punished'—long indeed have they been a trouble to the various Xia.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: the Odes pronounce the northern tribes fit for chastisement and the southern states of Jing and Shu fit for punishment—for ages they have plagued the heartland of civilization.

sentences.zh: 漢興，征伐胡越，於是為盛。

translations.literal: When Han rose, campaigning against Hu and Yue—thereby it became flourishing.
translations.idiomatic: With the rise of Han, wars against the steppe and the south reached their greatest intensity.

sentences.zh: 究觀淮南、捐之、主父、嚴安之義，深切著明，故備論其語。

translations.literal: Thoroughly observing the meanings of Huainan, Juanzhi, Zhufu, and Yan An—they are deep, cutting, clear, and manifest; therefore their words are fully discussed.
translations.idiomatic: The arguments of the Huainan faction, of Jia Juanzhi, of Zhufu Yan, and of Yan An repay close reading: they are trenchant, lucid, and morally plain, which is why this chapter records their words at such length.

sentences.zh: 世稱公孫弘排主父，張湯陷嚴助，石顯譖捐之，察其行跡，主父求欲鼎亨而得族，嚴、賈出入禁門招權利，死皆其所也，亦何排陷之恨哉！

translations.literal: The world calls Gongsun Hong having excluded Zhufu, Zhang Tang having trapped Yan Zhu, Shi Xian having slandered Juanzhi; inspecting their tracks and conduct, Zhufu sought the eminence of the minister's tripod and obtained clan execution; Yan and Jia passed in and out of the forbidden gate summoning power and profit—their deaths were each their proper lot—what resentment then about exclusion and entrapment!
translations.idiomatic: Posterity blames Gongsun Hong for sidelining Zhufu Yan, Zhang Tang for ruining Yan Zhu, and Shi Xian for destroying Jia Juanzhi—yet examine how these men lived. Zhufu Yan clawed after the honors of the high minister's tripod and instead won the execution of his entire kin; Yan Zhu and Jia Juanzhi used their passes through the forbidden gates to peddle influence and line their pockets. Each fell by his own doing; there is little cause to nurse a grudge against those who merely 'squeezed' or 'framed' them.
